# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, April 2024 Style]**

### Question A.1
**Define the term "DC current gain $\beta_{DC}$" for a BJT in CE configuration. How is it distinguished from the AC current gain $\beta_{AC}$?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

- **$\beta_{DC}$** is defined as the **ratio of the DC collector current to the DC base current** at a specific operating point:

$$\beta_{DC} = \frac{I_C}{I_B}\bigg|_{\text{Q-point}}$$

It is a **single-point ratio** obtained from the output characteristic at one chosen quiescent point (Q-point) in the active region.

- **$\beta_{AC}$** is defined as the **ratio of small change in collector current to the corresponding small change in base current** at a constant $V_{CE}$:

$$\beta_{AC} = \left.\frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B}\right|_{V_{CE} = \text{const}}$$

It represents the **slope** of the output characteristic in the active region and is also denoted as the **h-parameter $h_{fe}$**.

- The two differ because $\beta_{DC}$ is a static ratio while $\beta_{AC}$ is a dynamic (incremental) ratio. Generally, $\beta_{AC} > \beta_{DC}$, and the percentage difference is reported as experimental error.

*(1 mark for $\beta_{DC}$ definition, 1 mark for $\beta_{AC}$ definition, 1 mark for distinction.)*

---

### Question A.2
**Why does the input characteristic of a CE BJT shift slightly to the right when $V_{CE}$ is increased?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The shift is caused by the **Early Effect (base-width modulation)**:

1. As $V_{CE}$ increases, the **collector-base depletion region widens** (because the CB junction is reverse-biased by a larger voltage).
2. The **effective base width decreases** as the depletion region encroaches into the base.
3. This **reduces the recombination of charge carriers in the base**, which means **fewer electrons recombine** to form the base current.
4. Hence, for the same $V_{BE}$, the **base current $I_B$ decreases**.
5. To obtain the **same $I_B$**, a slightly **higher $V_{BE}$** is required, which **shifts the input characteristic curve to the right**.

*(1 mark for Early Effect identification, 1 mark for base-width modulation explanation, 1 mark for shift-to-right conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose ONE

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Lab-Based Theory Question Style]**

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Draw the **circuit diagram** of an NPN BJT in CE configuration used to obtain the input and output characteristics. Explain the **procedure** for obtaining the output characteristics step by step. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** From the output characteristics of a CE BJT, the following readings are recorded in the active region:

| $V_{CE}$ (V) | $I_C$ (mA) at $I_B = 30\,\mu$A | $I_C$ (mA) at $I_B = 50\,\mu$A |
|--------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| 3 | 1.65 | 2.75 |
| 7 | 1.78 | 2.95 |

Calculate:
1. The DC current gain $\beta_{DC}$ at $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$.
2. The AC current gain $\beta_{AC}$.
3. The output resistance $r_o$ at $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$.
4. The transconductance $g_m$ using $g_m = \beta_{AC}/h_{ie}$ where $h_{ie} = 2.5\text{ k}\Omega$.

**\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Circuit Diagram and Procedure \[7 Marks\]**

**Circuit Diagram:**

```
        VCC (+12 V)
          |
          |
         [RC = 1 kΩ]
          |
          |
          +----> to Ammeter (IC) and Voltmeter (VCE) ----> GND
          |
        COLLECTOR (C)
          |
         (NPN BJT — e.g., BC547)
          |
        BASE (B) <---- from RB = 470 kΩ ---- VBB (or VCC)
          |
          +----> to Ammeter (IB) and Voltmeter (VBE) ----> EMITTER (E)
                                                              |
                                                            GND (0 V)
```

The emitter is common to both input (B-E) and output (C-E) loops.

**Procedure for Output Characteristics \[5 marks for procedure; 2 marks for diagram\]:**

1. **Setup:** Make the circuit connections on the breadboard. Insert the BJT (e.g., BC547) with correct pin identification (E-B-C for BC547 viewed from the flat side).
2. **Set $V_{BB} = 0$ initially**, $V_{CC} = 0$, and switch on the power supply.
3. **Set $V_{CC} = 1\text{ V}$** (a constant low value to start).
4. **Vary $V_{BB}$** (through the base resistor $R_B$) until $I_B = 20\,\mu\text{A}$ (use the base ammeter to set this value precisely).
5. **Vary $V_{CC}$** from $0$ to $10\text{ V}$ in steps of $1\text{ V}$, and at each step record $V_{CE}$ and $I_C$ in the observation table.
6. **Repeat** for $I_B = 40\,\mu\text{A}, 60\,\mu\text{A}, 80\,\mu\text{A}, 100\,\mu\text{A}$ by adjusting $V_{BB}$.
7. **Plot** the graph of $I_C$ (y-axis) vs. $V_{CE}$ (x-axis) with $I_B$ as the parameter. Each curve corresponds to one fixed $I_B$.
8. **Identify** the cutoff region ($I_B = 0$ curve), saturation region (left steep portion), and active region (flat portion) on the plotted graph.

---

**Part (b) — Calculations \[7 Marks: 2+2+2+1\]**

**1. DC Current Gain $\beta_{DC}$ at $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$ (at $V_{CE} = 7\text{ V}$):**

$$\beta_{DC} = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{1.78 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\beta_{DC} = \frac{1.78 \times 10^{-3}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{DC} = 59.33 \approx 59}$$

**2. AC Current Gain $\beta_{AC}$:**

Using the two values at $V_{CE} = 7\text{ V}$:

$$\beta_{AC} = \frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B} = \frac{(2.95 - 1.78)\text{ mA}}{(50 - 30)\,\mu\text{A}}$$

$$\beta_{AC} = \frac{1.17 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.17 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{AC} = 58.5}$$

**3. Output Resistance $r_o$ at $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$:**

$$h_{oe} = \frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta V_{CE}} = \frac{(1.78 - 1.65) \times 10^{-3}}{7 - 3} = \frac{0.13 \times 10^{-3}}{4}$$

$$h_{oe} = 32.5 \times 10^{-6}\text{ S} = 32.5\,\mu\text{S}$$

$$r_o = \frac{1}{h_{oe}} = \frac{1}{32.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{r_o = 30{,}769\,\Omega \approx 30.77\text{ k}\Omega}$$

**4. Transconductance $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \frac{\beta_{AC}}{h_{ie}} = \frac{58.5}{2500}$$

$$\boxed{g_m = 0.0234\text{ S} = 23.4\text{ mS}}$$

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a **neatly labelled output characteristic** of a CE-connected NPN transistor, identify and explain the **three operating regions** — cutoff, active, and saturation. Mention the biasing condition of the B-E and C-B junctions in each region. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A silicon NPN transistor in CE configuration has the following readings in the active region at $V_{CE} = 6\text{ V}$:

| $I_B$ ($\mu$A) | 10 | 30 | 50 | 70 |
|----------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $I_C$ (mA) | 0.50 | 1.55 | 2.65 | 3.78 |

Calculate:
1. The DC current gain $\beta_{DC}$ at $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$.
2. The AC current gain $\beta_{AC}$ between $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$ and $I_B = 50\,\mu\text{A}$.
3. The base-emitter voltage $V_{BE}$ (assumed constant at $0.7\text{ V}$) and use it to estimate the **theoretical** value of $r_\pi = V_T / I_B$ at $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$, given $V_T = 26\text{ mV}$ and $\beta = 60$.
4. Comment on whether the transistor is operating in the **active**, **saturation**, or **cutoff** region.

**\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Three Operating Regions \[7 Marks\]**

The CE output characteristic is divided into three principal regions:

**(i) Cutoff Region** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Biasing:** Both B-E and C-B junctions are reverse-biased (or $I_B = 0$).
- **Current:** $I_C \approx 0$ (only leakage $I_{CEO}$ flows, on the order of nA).
- **Location on curve:** The $I_B = 0$ curve near the x-axis.
- **Analogy:** Transistor behaves like an **OPEN switch (OFF state)**.

**(ii) Active (Linear) Region** *(2 marks)*
- **Biasing:** B-E junction **forward-biased** ($V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$), C-B junction **reverse-biased** ($V_{CB} > 0$).
- **Current:** $I_C = \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B + I_{CEO} \approx \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B$; almost independent of $V_{CE}$ (very slight positive slope due to Early Effect).
- **Location on curve:** The flat, almost horizontal portion of each $I_B$ curve.
- **Analogy:** Transistor behaves like a **current-controlled current source** — used for **amplification**.

**(iii) Saturation Region** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Biasing:** Both B-E and C-B junctions are **forward-biased**.
- **Current:** $I_C$ rises very steeply (almost vertically) with $V_{CE}$.
- **Voltage:** $V_{CE} = V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx \mathbf{0.1\text{ V}}$ to $\mathbf{0.2\text{ V}}$ for silicon.
- **Location on curve:** Steep left-side portion of the output characteristic.
- **Analogy:** Transistor behaves like a **CLOSED switch (ON state)** — minimum voltage drop, maximum current flow.

*(Remaining 2 marks for labelled diagram and identifying boundary conditions like $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ and $V_{BE(\text{cut-in})}$.)*

---

**Part (b) — Calculations \[7 Marks: 2+2+2+1\]**

**1. DC Current Gain $\beta_{DC}$ at $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$, $V_{CE} = 6\text{ V}$:**

$$\beta_{DC} = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{1.55 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{DC} = \frac{1.55}{0.030} = 51.67 \approx 52}$$

**2. AC Current Gain $\beta_{AC}$ between $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$ and $I_B = 50\,\mu\text{A}$:**

$$\beta_{AC} = \frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B} = \frac{(2.65 - 1.55) \times 10^{-3}}{(50 - 30) \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\beta_{AC} = \frac{1.10 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.10 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{AC} = 55}$$

**3. Theoretical $r_\pi$ at $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$:**

First, calculate $I_C$ using the given $\beta = 60$:

$$I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = 60 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A} = 1.8\text{ mA}$$

Theoretical input resistance:

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta \cdot V_T}{I_C} = \frac{60 \times 26 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{1.56}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.56 \times 10^{3}}{1.8}$$

$$\boxed{r_\pi = 866.67\,\Omega \approx 867\,\Omega}$$

**4. Operating Region Comment:**

- The collector current $I_C = 1.55\text{ mA}$ at $I_B = 30\,\mu\text{A}$ is positive and substantial.
- $V_{CE} = 6\text{ V}$ is comfortably larger than $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$, so the C-B junction is reverse-biased.
- $I_C$ varies almost linearly with $I_B$ (check: $\beta_{AC} \approx \beta_{DC}$, both around 50–55).

**Conclusion:** The transistor is operating in the **Active (Linear) Region**. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Lab Report Evaluation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\beta_{DC}$ and $\beta_{AC}$:** Many students use a single formula for both. Always state explicitly *which* $\beta$ you are computing and *where* the readings come from (one point vs. slope).
>
> 2. **Forgetting units:** $I_B$ is in $\mu$A, $I_C$ is in mA. Convert both to **Amperes** before dividing. Marks are deducted for raw unit mismatch.
>
> 3. **Reading graphs incorrectly:** Use a **sharp pencil** and **ruler** to read coordinates from the plotted graph. Reading at a **non-flat portion** of the active region (i.e., near saturation or breakdown) gives wrong values.
>
> 4. **Not plotting the load line:** In lab reports, **always draw the DC load line** $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ on the output characteristic and **mark the Q-point**. Marks are reserved for this.
>
> 5. **Missing the "active region" qualifier:** When asked to find $\beta$, state that the readings must be taken **in the active region** (flat portion of the curve), not in saturation or cutoff.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Early Effect:** When asked why curves are not perfectly flat, mention the **Early Effect** and the concept of **output resistance $r_o$**.
>
> 7. **Using wrong terminal:** Some students connect the ammeter in the wrong branch (e.g., emitter instead of collector). The $I_C$ value will then be off by a factor of $\beta+1 \approx I_E$.
>
> 8. **Not reporting percentage error:** Always compute and report the **% error** between graphical and theoretical values of parameters — it is a **mandatory part** of KTU lab record evaluation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Save This Section for Exam Day**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ **BJT** = Bipolar Junction Transistor = current-controlled current device with three terminals (Base, Collector, Emitter).
- ✅ **CE configuration** = emitter is common to input and output; most widely used configuration.
- ✅ **Input characteristics** = plot of $I_B$ vs. $V_{BE}$ at constant $V_{CE}$.
- ✅ **Output characteristics** = plot of $I_C$ vs. $V_{CE}$ for several constant values of $I_B$.

### Four Operating Regions of Output Characteristic
- ✅ **Cutoff:** $I_B = 0$, $I_C \approx 0$ — both junctions reverse-biased.
- ✅ **Active (Linear):** $I_C = \beta I_B$ — B-E forward, C-B reverse — **amplification region**.
- ✅ **Saturation:** $V_{CE} = V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$ — both junctions forward-biased — **switch ON**.
- ✅ **Breakdown:** $V_{CE} > V_{(BR)CEO}$ — must be avoided — **destructive**.

### Key Formulas (Minimum Memorization Set)
- ✅ $\beta_{DC} = I_C / I_B$ (single point)
- ✅ $\beta_{AC} = \Delta I_C / \Delta I_B$ (slope, $h_{fe}$)
- ✅ $h_{ie} = \Delta V_{BE} / \Delta I_B$ (input resistance, at constant $V_{CE}$)
- ✅ $h_{oe} = \Delta I_C / \Delta V_{CE}$ (output conductance, at constant $I_B$)
- ✅ $r_o = 1/h_{oe}$ (output resistance)
- ✅ $g_m = \beta_{AC} / h_{ie} = I_C / V_T$ (transconductance)
- ✅ $V_T \approx 26\text{ mV}$ at 300 K

### Biasing Conditions — Quick Table
| Region | B-E Junction | C-B Junction | Application |
|--------|-------------|--------------|-------------|
| Cutoff | Reverse | Reverse | Switch OFF |
| Active | Forward | Reverse | **Amplifier** |
| Saturation | Forward | Forward | Switch ON |

### Standard Silicon Values (Memorize)
- ✅ $V_{BE(\text{active})} = \mathbf{0.7\text{ V}}$
- ✅ $V_{CE(\text{sat})} = \mathbf{0.2\text{ V}}$
- ✅ $V_{\gamma(\text{cut-in})} = \mathbf{0.5\text{ V}}$

### Practical Lab Tips
- ✅ Always identify BJT pins **before powering**.
- ✅ Use **separate ammeters** for $I_B$ (microammeter) and $I_C$ (milliammeter) ranges.
- ✅ Start with **$V_{BB} = 0$** and **$V_{CC} = 0$**, then ramp up slowly.
- ✅ Limit $I_C$ below the **maximum rating** (typically 100–800 mA).
- ✅ Plot the **DC load line** and **mark Q-point** on output characteristic.

### Common Viva-Voce Questions
- ✅ What is the Early Effect? → Base-width modulation due to reverse-biased CB junction.
- ✅ Why is CE configuration preferred over CB? → Higher current and voltage gain.
- ✅ What does $h_{fe}$ represent physically? → AC current gain (small-signal forward current transfer ratio).
- ✅ What is $\alpha$ vs. $\beta$? → $\alpha$ = CB current gain ($<1$); $\beta$ = CE current gain (large).
- ✅ Why does the output curve tilt upward slightly with $V_{CE}$? → Finite output resistance $r_o$ due to Early Effect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of JFET (Input and output characteristics)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL407 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of JFET (Input and output characteristics)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of JFET

The **Junction Field Effect Transistor (JFET)** is a three-terminal, **unipolar**, **voltage-controlled** semiconductor device in which the output current (drain current, $I_D$) flowing between the Drain (D) and Source (S) terminals is regulated by the **reverse-biased electric field** applied at the Gate (G) terminal.

The two principal variants of JFET are:
- **N-channel JFET** — majority carriers are **electrons**; conventional drain current flows from D to S.
- **P-channel JFET** — majority carriers are **holes**; conventional drain current flows from S to D.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from BJT:** A BJT is a *current-controlled* device requiring a continuous base current $I_B$, whereas a JFET is a *voltage-controlled* device where the gate draws **virtually zero current** (only the leakage current of the reverse-biased PN junction). This makes the JFET's **input impedance extremely high** (typically $10^8$ to $10^{12} \ \Omega$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A JFET is a **"Normally-ON" device** — at $V_{GS} = 0 \ \text{V}$, a conducting channel exists and maximum drain current $I_{DSS}$ flows. The channel is "pinched off" only when $V_{GS}$ is made sufficiently negative (for N-channel) to deplete the channel completely.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Squeeze Valve" Model

Imagine a **flexible rubber water pipe** carrying water from a tank to a tap.

| JFET Element | Water Pipe Analogy | Function |
|--------------|--------------------|----------|
| Drain (D) | Water inlet (high-pressure side) | Where carriers enter the channel |
| Source (S) | Water outlet (low-pressure side) | Where carriers leave the channel |
| Gate (G) | A mechanical clamp around the pipe | Squeezes the pipe to constrict flow |
| $V_{GS}$ | How tightly the clamp is tightened | The more negative $V_{GS}$, the tighter the squeeze |
| $V_{DS}$ | The water pressure pushing flow | Drives carriers through the channel |
| $I_D$ | The water flow rate | Output current controlled by gate voltage |

**Reading the analogy:** The clamp never *touches* the water (gate current is essentially zero — only a tiny reverse leakage), but it controls the flow by *squeezing* the pipe from the outside. Just as you cannot completely stop a stiff pipe by hand-clamping (there's always *some* flow), the JFET only fully cuts off at a specific negative voltage called $V_{GS(\text{off})}$ or $V_P$.

### 1.3 Terminal Identification & Symbol Conventions

**N-Channel JFET** (arrows point **inward** at the gate; channel is N-type):
- Gate is **P-type** material
- Channel is **N-type** material  
- Operating $V_{GS}$ range: $V_{GS(\text{off})} \le V_{GS} \le 0$ (negative)
- Operating $V_{DS}$: **positive** (Drain positive with respect to Source)

**P-Channel JFET** (arrows point **outward** at the gate; channel is P-type):
- Gate is **N-type** material
- Channel is **P-type** material
- Operating $V_{GS}$ range: $0 \le V_{GS} \le V_{GS(\text{off})}$ (positive)
- Operating $V_{DS}$: **negative** (Drain negative with respect to Source)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Channel Pinch-Off

When a reverse-bias voltage $V_{GS}$ is applied to the gate-channel junction:
1. The **depletion region** widens into the channel.
2. The effective cross-sectional area for current flow **shrinks**.
3. The channel resistance **increases**; $I_D$ **decreases**.
4. When depletion regions from both sides of the channel **meet**, the channel is said to be **pinched off**.

The voltage at which complete pinch-off occurs at $V_{DS} = 0$ is called the **Pinch-Off Voltage** $V_P$ (also called $V_{GS(\text{off})}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Pinch-off in JFETs does **NOT** mean current becomes zero. It is a saturation condition where the channel is narrowed to its minimum width, and $I_D$ saturates to a value determined solely by $V_{GS}$ — not by $V_{DS}$.

### 2.2 The Three Regions of Operation

| Region | Condition | Physical Behaviour | Equivalent Circuit |
|--------|-----------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Ohmic (Triode)** | $0 < V_{DS} < V_{DS}(\text{sat})$ | Channel acts as a voltage-controlled resistor; $I_D$ rises linearly with $V_{DS}$ | Variable resistor $r_{DS}$ |
| **Saturation (Pinch-off / Active)** | $V_{DS} \ge V_{DS}(\text{sat})$ | Channel is pinched; $I_D$ is almost constant, controlled by $V_{GS}$ | Current source $I_D$ with $r_d$ in parallel |
| **Cut-off** | $V_{GS} \le V_{GS(\text{off})}$ | Channel is fully depleted; $I_D \approx 0$ | Open circuit |

Where $V_{DS}(\text{sat}) = V_{GS} - V_P$ (since $V_P$ is negative for N-channel, this is a positive quantity).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Typical Values | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Shockley's Equation** | $I_D = I_{DSS} \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$ | $I_D$ in mA | Drain current in saturation region |
| 2 | **Maximum Drain Current** | $I_{DSS}$ | mA (e.g., 5–20 mA) | Drain current when $V_{GS} = 0$ |
| 3 | **Pinch-off Voltage** | $V_P \equiv V_{GS(\text{off})}$ | Volts (negative for N-JFET, e.g., $-2$ to $-10$ V) | Gate-source voltage to cut off $I_D$ |
| 4 | **Transconductance** | $g_m = \dfrac{2 \, I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ | Siemens (S) or mA/V | Small-signal gain parameter |
| 5 | **Transconductance at $V_{GS}=0$** | $g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 \, I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$ | Siemens (S) | Maximum $g_m$ |
| 6 | **Output (Drain) Resistance** | $r_d = \dfrac{\Delta V_{DS}}{\Delta I_D} \Big\vert_{V_{GS}}$ | $\text{k}\Omega$ to $\text{M}\Omega$ | Slope of $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve in saturation |
| 7 | **Amplification Factor** | $\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$ | dimensionless | Combined gain figure |
| 8 | **Saturation Drain Voltage** | $V_{DS}(\text{sat}) = V_{GS} - V_P$ | Volts | Onset of saturation region |
| 9 | **Input Resistance** | $R_{IN} \approx \dfrac{V_{GS}}{I_{GSS}}$ | $\ge 10^8 \ \Omega$ | Extremely high (reverse-biased gate) |
| 10 | **DC Drain Resistance** | $R_D = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{DS}}{I_D}$ | $\Omega$ | Used in bias calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, $\vert V_P \vert$ denotes the **magnitude** (absolute value) of the pinch-off voltage. For an N-channel JFET with $V_P = -5 \ \text{V}$, $\vert V_P \vert = 5 \ \text{V}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-impedance front-end amplifiers** (electrometer, oscilloscope input stages, microphone pre-amps) — leverage the enormous $R_{IN}$ of the JFET.
- **Source followers** (buffers) — used as impedance transformers between high-impedance sensors and low-impedance loads.
- **RF amplifier stages** — low noise figure of JFETs makes them ideal for VHF/UHF receiver front ends.
- **Constant-current sources** — exploit the saturation region to set a fixed $I_D$ by fixing $V_{GS}$.
- **Choppers and analog switches** — the JFET's low ON-resistance and high OFF-resistance make it suitable for switching applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Shockley's Equation: $I_D = I_{DSS}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)^2$

**Starting Assumptions:**
- The JFET operates in the **saturation (pinch-off) region**.
- The channel is a **GCA (Gradual Channel Approximation)** where depletion width varies slowly along its length.
- The channel has uniform doping concentration $N_D$ and mobility $\mu_n$.

**Step 1 — Channel Conductance at Position $x$:**

At any point $x$ along the channel, the channel height (effective thickness) is $h(x)$. The drain current at this point is given by Ohm's law for the channel:

$$I_D = q \cdot N_D \cdot \mu_n \cdot h(x) \cdot W \cdot \frac{dV(x)}{dx}$$

where $W$ is the channel width, $q$ is the electronic charge, and $V(x)$ is the channel potential at position $x$.

**Step 2 — Effective Channel Height under Reverse Bias:**

The reverse-bias voltage at position $x$ (between gate and that point in the channel) is $V(x) - V_{GS}$. The depletion width on the channel side grows as the square root of this reverse bias:

$$h(x) = h_0 \left[1 - \left(\frac{V(x) - V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{1/2}\right]$$

where $h_0$ is the channel thickness at $V_{GS} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Boundary Conditions:**

At the source end ($x = 0$): $V(0) = 0$
At the drain end ($x = L$): $V(L) = V_{DS}$

**Step 4 — Integration Across the Channel:**

Substituting and integrating from $x = 0$ to $x = L$, after algebraic manipulation the result is:

$$I_D = G_0 \left[V_{DS} - \frac{2}{3 V_P^{1/2}}\left[(V_{DS} - V_{GS})^{3/2} - (-V_{GS})^{3/2}\right]\right]$$

where $G_0$ is the channel conductance at $V_{GS} = 0$, $V_{DS} = 0$.

**Step 5 — Apply the Saturation Condition $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_P$:**

Substituting the saturation condition (where the channel is just pinched off at the drain end):

$$I_D = G_0 \left[(V_{GS} - V_P) - \frac{2}{3 V_P^{1/2}}\left[V_P^{3/2} - (-V_{GS})^{3/2}\right]\right]$$

**Step 6 — Simplification to Shockley's Canonical Form:**

After extensive algebraic simplification (the details are tedious but verifiable in any standard textbook), the expression reduces to the elegant **parabolic form**:

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation** of the JFET in the saturation region.

> [!NOTE]
> The parabola opens from the point $(V_P, 0)$ to the point $(0, I_{DSS})$, showing that $I_D$ varies as the **square** of the gate-source overdrive voltage. This is the basis of the **square-law nature** of JFETs and is critical for low-distortion amplifier design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Transconductance $g_m$

The transconductance is defined as the small-signal change in drain current per unit change in gate-source voltage, with $V_{DS}$ held constant:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \bigg\vert_{V_{DS} = \text{const}}$$

**Step 1 — Start with Shockley's Equation:**

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $V_{GS}$:**

Treating $I_{DSS}$ and $V_P$ as constants, and using the chain rule:

$$g_m = I_{DSS} \cdot 2\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{V_P}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Sign:**

For an N-channel JFET, $V_P$ is **negative**. The factor $(-1/V_P)$ thus becomes positive when written as $(1/|V_P|)$:

$$g_m = \frac{2 \, I_{DSS}}{|V_P|}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Define the Maximum Transconductance $g_{m0}$:**

At $V_{GS} = 0$:

$$g_{m0} = \frac{2 \, I_{DSS}}{|V_P|}$$

**Step 5 — Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)}$$

This shows that $g_m$ **decreases linearly** as $V_{GS}$ is made more negative — physically, the JFET becomes a less effective amplifier as the channel narrows.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Output Resistance $r_d$

The output (drain) resistance is defined in the saturation region as:

$$r_d = \frac{\partial V_{DS}}{\partial I_D} \bigg\vert_{V_{GS} = \text{const}}$$

**Step 1 — Practical Approximation:**

Since $I_D$ is nearly independent of $V_{DS}$ in saturation, the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve has a **very small positive slope**. The reciprocal of this slope is $r_d$.

**Step 2 — Operational Definition (from experimental graph):**

$$r_d = \frac{\Delta V_{DS}}{\Delta I_D} \bigg\vert_{V_{GS} = \text{const}}$$

**Step 3 — Typical Magnitude:**

For small-signal JFETs, $r_d$ ranges from **$10 \ \text{k}\Omega$** (low-power devices) to over **$1 \ \text{M}\Omega$** (high-quality devices). This high $r_d$ is precisely what gives the JFET amplifier its high voltage gain when loaded with an appropriate drain resistor.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A particular N-channel JFET has $I_{DSS} = 8 \ \text{mA}$ and $V_P = -4 \ \text{V}$. Calculate (a) $I_D$ at $V_{GS} = -1 \ \text{V}$, (b) $g_m$ at $V_{GS} = -1 \ \text{V}$, (c) $V_{DS}(\text{sat})$ at $V_{GS} = -1 \ \text{V}$.

**Solution (a):**

$$I_D = 8 \left(1 - \frac{-1}{-4}\right)^2 = 8 \left(1 - 0.25\right)^2 = 8 \times 0.5625 = 4.5 \ \text{mA}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$g_m = \frac{2 \times 8}{4}\left(1 - \frac{-1}{-4}\right) = 4 \times 0.75 = 3.0 \ \text{mA/V}$$

**Solution (c):**

$$V_{DS}(\text{sat}) = V_{GS} - V_P = -1 - (-4) = 3 \ \text{V}$$

For $V_{DS} \ge 3 \ \text{V}$, the JFET is in saturation and the value $I_D = 4.5 \ \text{mA}$ holds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — JFET DC Biasing & Measurement Circuit

The following diagram represents the **functional block-level architecture** of the experimental setup used to plot the characteristics. (This is a *block diagram* rather than a physical schematic, per the safe-handling rule for complex electronic circuits.)

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD[V_DD POWER SUPPLY POSITIVE RAIL] --> RESD[DRAIN RESISTOR R_D]
    RESD --> DRAIN[DRAIN TERMINAL OF JFET]
    JFETCENTER[JFET N CHANNEL DEVICE] --> DRAIN
    JFETCENTER --> SOURCE[SOURCE TERMINAL GROUNDED]
    VGG[V_GG GATE BIAS SUPPLY NEGATIVE FOR N CHANNEL] --> RESG[GATE RESISTOR R_G]
    RESG --> GATE[GATE TERMINAL OF JFET]
    VMETERD[V_METER D VOLTMETER ACROSS DRAIN TO SOURCE] --> JFETCENTER
    VMETERG[V_METER G VOLTMETER ACROSS GATE TO SOURCE] --> JFETCENTER
    IMETER[I_METER AMMETER IN DRAIN LEAD] --> RESD
```

**Reading the block diagram:**

- **$V_{DD}$** (positive) feeds the drain through a current-limiting/protective resistor $R_D$ and an ammeter $I_M$.
- **$V_{GG}$** (negative for N-channel) feeds the gate through a high-value resistor $R_G$ (typically $1 \ \text{M}\Omega$) — the high $R_G$ ensures the gate draws negligible current.
- The **voltmeter $V_M(G)$** measures $V_{GS}$ (gate-to-source voltage).
- The **voltmeter $V_M(D)$** measures $V_{DS}$ (drain-to-source voltage).
- The **ammeter $I_M$** measures the drain current $I_D$.

By varying $V_{GG}$ and $V_{DD}$ step by step and recording $(V_{GS}, I_D)$ and $(V_{DS}, I_D)$ pairs, the **input/transfer characteristics** and the **output/drain characteristics** are obtained.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Experimental Procedure for Plotting Characteristics

```mermaid
graph TD
    STEP0[STEP 0 POWER ON SUPPLIES SET V_DD TO 0V] --> STEP1
    STEP1[STEP 1 SET V_GG TO 0V MEASURE I_D EQUALS I_DSS] --> STEP2
    STEP2[STEP 2 APPLY V_GG EQUALS MINUS 0.5V] --> STEP3
    STEP3[STEP 3 APPLY V_DD IN STEPS OF 1V FROM 0 TO 10V] --> STEP4
    STEP4[STEP 4 RECORD I_D FOR EACH V_DS AT THIS V_GS] --> STEP5
    STEP5[STEP 5 REPEAT FOR V_GS EQUALS MINUS 1V, MINUS 1.5V, MINUS 2V, V_P] --> STEP6
    STEP6[STEP 6 PLOT I_D VS V_DS CURVES FOR EACH V_GS OUTPUT CHARACTERISTICS] --> STEP7
    STEP7[STEP 7 FROM DATA PLOT I_D VS V_GS AT A FIXED V_DS IN SATURATION INPUT CHARACTERISTICS] --> STEP8
    STEP8[STEP 8 EXTRACT I_DSS, V_P, g_m0 FROM CURVES] --> DONE[EXPERIMENT COMPLETE]
```

---

### 4.3 Expected Output Characteristics (Description of the Family of Curves)

| Curve Index | $V_{GS}$ Setting | Behaviour |
|-------------|-------------------|-----------|
| Topmost curve | $V_{GS} = 0$ | Highest $I_D$ ($= I_{DSS} = 8$ mA in our example); $V_{DS}(\text{sat}) = \vert V_P \vert = 4$ V |
| Next curve | $V_{GS} = -1$ V | $I_D = 4.5$ mA; $V_{DS}(\text{sat}) = 3$ V |
| Next curve | $V_{GS} = -2$ V | $I_D = 2$ mA; $V_{DS}(\text{sat}) = 2$ V |
| Lower curve | $V_{GS} = -3$ V | $I_D = 0.5$ mA; $V_{DS}(\text{sat}) = 1$ V |
| Bottom curve | $V_{GS} = V_P = -4$ V | $I_D = 0$ (cut-off); the curve collapses to the $V_{DS}$ axis |

In the **ohmic region** (left of the saturation knee), all curves rise steeply from the origin. In the **saturation region** (right of the knee), the curves are nearly horizontal — a hallmark of JFET behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the terms (i) Pinch-off voltage $V_P$, and (ii) Drain current at zero gate bias $I_{DSS}$, for an N-channel JFET. How are they related to each other?*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Pinch-off voltage $V_P$ (also called $V_{GS(\text{off})}$):** It is the value of the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ at which the drain current $I_D$ is reduced to **zero**. At this reverse bias, the depletion regions extending from the gate-channel junctions on both sides meet at the centre, completely closing the channel. For an N-channel JFET, $V_P$ is **negative**; for a P-channel JFET, $V_P$ is **positive**.

**(ii) $I_{DSS}$ (Drain current with Source Shorted):** It is the drain current that flows when the gate is **shorted-circuited to the source** (i.e., $V_{GS} = 0$ V) and a specified $V_{DS}$ (typically in saturation) is applied. It represents the **maximum** possible drain current of the JFET.

**Relation:** They are connected through **Shockley's equation**:

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

At $V_{GS} = V_P$, the bracket becomes zero and $I_D = 0$; at $V_{GS} = 0$, $I_D = I_{DSS}$. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the input resistance of a JFET very high compared to a BJT? Mention any two advantages and one disadvantage of a JFET.*

**Model Answer:**

The input resistance of a JFET is extremely high because its gate is **reverse-biased** with respect to the channel under normal operation. A reverse-biased PN junction draws only a **negligible leakage current** (typically in nA or pA range), and the ratio of applied $V_{GS}$ to this leakage current yields a very high input resistance (typically $10^8$ to $10^{12} \ \Omega$). In contrast, the BJT's base-emitter junction is **forward-biased**, allowing large base current to flow, giving a low input resistance (a few k$\Omega$).

**Two advantages:**
1. **High input impedance** — minimal loading effect on the previous stage; ideal for sensor interfacing.
2. **Low noise figure** — majority-carrier conduction generates less noise than BJT's minority-carrier recombination; excellent for RF amplifiers.

**One disadvantage:**
- **Low transconductance** and hence lower voltage gain compared to a BJT at similar operating point, because the JFET's square-law characteristic produces smaller $g_m$ values. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> **Internal Choice Convention:** In KTU ESE, the student may attempt **either** OR-style choice. Both alternatives are given below.

---

###  Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3a. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
*(7 marks)* With the help of a neat circuit diagram, describe the experimental procedure to obtain the **drain (output) characteristics** of an N-channel JFET. Draw the expected family of curves and clearly label the three regions of operation.

**Model Answer (Q3a):**

**Circuit Description:** The experimental setup consists of an N-channel JFET with:
- A variable **$V_{DD}$** (drain supply, 0 to +15 V) connected to the drain through a protective resistor $R_D$ and a DC ammeter $I_M$ (to measure $I_D$).
- A variable **$V_{GG}$** (gate supply, 0 to $-10$ V) connected to the gate through a high-value resistor $R_G \approx 1 \ \text{M}\Omega$ (to set $V_{GS}$).
- A **voltmeter $V_{M(G)}$** across gate and source to measure $V_{GS}$.
- A **voltmeter $V_{M(D)}$** across drain and source to measure $V_{DS}$.
- The source terminal is **grounded**.

**Procedure:**

1. Initially set $V_{DD} = 0$ V and $V_{GG} = 0$ V.
2. Set $V_{GG} = 0$ V (gate shorted to source). With $V_{GS} = 0$, vary $V_{DD}$ in steps of 1 V from 0 V to about 10 V. Record the corresponding $I_D$ and $V_{DS}$. This yields the **topmost curve** of the family.
3. Set $V_{GG} = -0.5$ V. Repeat the variation of $V_{DD}$ and tabulate $(V_{DS}, I_D)$. This yields the second curve.
4. Repeat for $V_{GG} = -1$ V, $-1.5$ V, $-2$ V, …, until $I_D$ becomes zero (this is the cut-off case at $V_{GS} = V_P$).
5. Plot $I_D$ (y-axis) versus $V_{DS}$ (x-axis) for each value of $V_{GS}$.

**Expected Family of Curves and Three Regions:**

- **Ohmic / Triode Region** (left of the knee): $V_{DS} < V_{DS}(\text{sat})$ — all curves rise steeply and almost linearly from the origin. The JFET behaves as a **voltage-controlled resistor**.
- **Saturation (Pinch-off / Active) Region** (right of the knee): $V_{DS} \ge V_{DS}(\text{sat})$ — the curves become **almost flat horizontal lines**. $I_D$ is now controlled by $V_{GS}$ alone, per Shockley's equation. The **knee point** shifts leftward as $V_{GS}$ becomes more negative.
- **Cut-off Region** (the lowest curve at $V_{GS} = V_P$): $I_D = 0$ for all $V_{DS}$.

**Key observations to record:**
- The curve for $V_{GS} = 0$ gives $I_{DSS}$ (the maximum drain current).
- The point where successive curves intersect the $V_{DS}$ axis gives $V_{DS}(\text{sat})$ for that $V_{GS}$.
- The point where $I_D$ first becomes zero is $V_P$ (pinch-off voltage). (7 marks)

---

**Q3b. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
*(7 marks)* From the output characteristics of an N-channel JFET, explain how the **mutual characteristic (transfer characteristic)** is obtained. Show that this curve is a **parabola** described by Shockley's equation.

**Model Answer (Q3b):**

The **transfer (mutual) characteristic** of a JFET is the plot of $I_D$ versus $V_{GS}$ at a **fixed $V_{DS}$ in the saturation region**. It is derived directly from the output characteristics:

1. On the output characteristics family, draw a **vertical line at a chosen $V_{DS}$** (in the saturation region, say $V_{DS} = 5$ V for our example JFET with $V_P = -4$ V).
2. This vertical line **intersects each output curve** at a specific point, giving one $(V_{GS}, I_D)$ pair per curve.
3. These intersection points, when re-plotted with $V_{GS}$ on the x-axis and $I_D$ on the y-axis, form the **transfer characteristic**.

**Mathematical Derivation (showing parabolic nature):**

In the saturation region, the drain current depends only on $V_{GS}$ (not on $V_{DS}$). The functional relationship is **Shockley's equation**:

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

Let us define a normalised variable $x = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$. Then:

$$I_D = I_{DSS} \cdot x^2$$

This is the **equation of a parabola** in the variable $x$. As $V_{GS}$ varies from $0$ to $V_P$:

- At $V_{GS} = 0$: $x = 1$, so $I_D = I_{DSS}$ (maximum).
- At $V_{GS} = V_P$: $x = 0$, so $I_D = 0$ (cut-off).
- For intermediate $V_{GS}$: $I_D$ varies as the **square** of the normalised voltage — a parabolic curve.

The transfer curve is therefore a **right-opening parabola** passing through $(0, I_{DSS})$ and $(V_P, 0)$. Because of the square-law dependence, the curve is **flatter** near $V_{GS} = 0$ and **steeper** near $V_{GS} = V_P$, making the JFET's gain vary considerably over its operating range. (7 marks)

---

###  Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4a. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
*(7 marks)* An N-channel JFET has the following parameters: $I_{DSS} = 10$ mA, $V_P = -5$ V. Calculate the drain current $I_D$ for (i) $V_{GS} = 0$ V, (ii) $V_{GS} = -2$ V, (iii) $V_{GS} = -4$ V. Also find the transconductance $g_m$ for case (ii).

**Model Answer (Q4a):**

**Shockley's Equation:**

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2 = 10\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{-5}\right)^2 \ \text{mA}$$

**Case (i): $V_{GS} = 0$ V**

$$I_D = 10\left(1 - 0\right)^2 = 10 \ \text{mA} = I_{DSS} \ \checkmark$$

**Case (ii): $V_{GS} = -2$ V**

$$I_D = 10\left(1 - \frac{-2}{-5}\right)^2 = 10\left(1 - 0.4\right)^2 = 10 \times 0.36 = 3.6 \ \text{mA}$$

**Transconductance at $V_{GS} = -2$ V:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 \times I_{DSS}}{|V_P|}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) = \frac{2 \times 10}{5}\left(1 - 0.4\right) = 4 \times 0.6 = 2.4 \ \text{mA/V}$$

**Case (iii): $V_{GS} = -4$ V**

$$I_D = 10\left(1 - \frac{-4}{-5}\right)^2 = 10\left(1 - 0.8\right)^2 = 10 \times 0.04 = 0.4 \ \text{mA}$$

**Tabulated Summary:**

| $V_{GS}$ (V) | $I_D$ (mA) | $g_m$ (mA/V) |
|:------------:|:----------:|:------------:|
| $0$          | $10$       | $4.0$        |
| $-2$         | $3.6$      | $2.4$        |
| $-4$         | $0.4$      | $0.4$        |

(7 marks)

---

**Q4b. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
*(7 marks)* Explain the construction of an N-channel JFET with a neat sketch. Describe how the channel is pinched off and discuss the difference between the **pinch-off in JFET** and **saturation in BJT**.

**Model Answer (Q4b):**

**Construction of N-channel JFET:**

An N-channel JFET is constructed as follows:
- A bar of **N-type semiconductor** (the channel) with two ohmic contacts at its ends — the **Source (S)** and the **Drain (D)**.
- On either side of the channel, two regions of **P-type semiconductor** are diffused (or grown), forming a single P-type **gate (G)** region. (Some designs use two separate gates; the standard JFET has one contiguous gate.)
- The P-N junctions between the P-type gate and the N-type channel are the **gate-channel junctions**.
- The whole structure is housed in a metal/ceramic package with three leads brought out for S, D, and G.

**Pinch-off Mechanism:**

When the gate is reverse-biased with respect to the source (i.e., $V_{GS}$ is negative for N-channel), the depletion regions around the two gate-channel junctions **widen and extend into the channel** from both sides. As $V_{GS}$ becomes more negative:
1. The depletion widths grow.
2. The effective cross-sectional area of the channel **shrinks**.
3. The channel resistance **increases**, reducing $I_D$.
4. When the two depletion regions from either side **touch at the centre** of the channel, the channel is **pinched off**. The voltage at which this happens is $V_P$.

**Difference between JFET pinch-off and BJT saturation:**

| Aspect | JFET Pinch-off | BJT Saturation |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Carrier flow** | Current is **not zero**; $I_D$ is finite and nearly constant | Both junctions forward-biased; $V_{CE}$ is very small (typically $0.2$ V); $I_C$ is **maximum** for given $I_B$ |
| **Effect on current** | $I_D$ **saturates to a constant value** controlled by $V_{GS}$ | $I_C$ is still controlled by $I_B$; the device is "ON" maximally |
| **Physical meaning** | Channel is **narrowed to minimum width** but carriers still drift through | Both junctions are **forward-biased**; collector is no longer collecting efficiently |
| **Useful region** | Saturation region is the **active / amplification region** of the JFET | Saturation is a **switching state** (transistor is fully ON) — **not** an amplification state |
| **Name confusion** | "Pinch-off" in JFET = the **active region** where amplification occurs | "Saturation" in BJT = the **switched-ON state**, not the amplification region (active region in BJT = forward-active region) |

(7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Confusing the polarity of $V_P$** — students often write $V_P = +5$ V for an N-channel JFET, but it must be **negative** because the gate-channel junction is reverse-biased to pinch off the channel. Always state the sign explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up Shockley's equation with BJT's $I_C$ equation** — the JFET's $I_D$ varies as the **square** of $(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)$, not exponentially as in the BJT. The square-law nature is the JFET's signature.
> 3. **Forgetting to square the bracket** — a frequent slip is to write $I_D = I_{DSS}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)$ (linear) instead of the correct quadratic form. This is an instant **1-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Confusing $I_D$ with $I_{DSS}$** — $I_D$ is the variable drain current at a chosen $V_{GS}$; $I_{DSS}$ is the specific value at $V_{GS} = 0$ V. Both must be quoted with the correct subscript.
> 5. **Drawing the family of curves without labelling the three regions** (ohmic, saturation, cut-off) — KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for unlabelled graphs.
> 6. **Forgetting the units** of $g_m$ (mA/V or S) and $V_{DS}(\text{sat})$ (V) — write the units in the final answer box.
> 7. **Mixing up "input characteristics" with "output characteristics"** — the **input/transfer characteristic** is the plot of $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$ (a parabola), while the **output/drain characteristic** is the plot of $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ for different $V_{GS}$ (a family of curves). Examiners check the labels carefully.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **JFET** = **J**unction **F**ield-**E**ffect **T**ransistor; a **unipolar**, **voltage-controlled**, **normally-ON** device.
- Three terminals: **Drain (D), Source (S), Gate (G)**.
- Two types: **N-channel** (channel is N-type, $V_P < 0$, $V_{DS} > 0$) and **P-channel** (channel is P-type, $V_P > 0$, $V_{DS} < 0$).
- **Pinch-off voltage $V_P$**: the $V_{GS}$ at which $I_D = 0$. For N-channel, $V_P$ is **negative**.
- **$I_{DSS}$**: the maximum drain current, obtained at $V_{GS} = 0$ V with drain in saturation.
- **Shockley's Equation**: $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$ — the **fundamental governing law** of the JFET in saturation.
- **Transconductance** $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{|V_P|}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ — quantifies the JFET's small-signal gain.
- **Three operating regions**: (i) **Ohmic** ($V_{DS} < V_{DS}(\text{sat})$), (ii) **Saturation/Active** ($V_{DS} \ge V_{DS}(\text{sat})$), (iii) **Cut-off** ($V_{GS} \le V_P$).
- **Saturation drain voltage**: $V_{DS}(\text{sat}) = V_{GS} - V_P$.
- **Amplification factor** $\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$ (dimensionless).
- **Input characteristic** (transfer curve): $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$ at fixed $V_{DS}$ in saturation — a **parabola**.
- **Output characteristic** (drain curve): $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ for various $V_{GS}$ — a **family of curves** with the topmost at $V_{GS} = 0$ and the bottommost collapsing at $V_{GS} = V_P$.
- **High input resistance** ($10^8$ to $10^{12} \ \Omega$) due to the reverse-biased gate; this is the JFET's biggest advantage over the BJT.
- **JFET pinch-off** $\ne$ **BJT saturation** — pinch-off is the JFET's amplification region; BJT saturation is its fully-ON switching state.
- **Practical uses**: high-impedance amplifiers, RF front ends, source followers, analog switches, constant-current sources.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and implement Inverting op-amp, non inverting op-amp, and voltage follower using op-amp. Plot the frequency response curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL407 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and implement Inverting op-amp, non inverting op-amp, and voltage follower using op-amp. Plot the frequency response curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) Configurations:** An op-amp is a high-gain, direct-coupled electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs (inverting `(-)` and non-inverting `(+)`) and a single-ended output. The three fundamental closed-loop configurations studied in this experiment are the **Inverting Amplifier**, **Non-Inverting Amplifier**, and **Voltage Follower (Unity Gain Buffer)**. Each configuration is realized by applying **negative feedback** through an external resistor network, which sets a precise, predictable closed-loop voltage gain ($A_v$).

In the **Inverting Configuration**, the input signal is applied to the inverting terminal, producing an output that is **180° out of phase** with the input.

In the **Non-Inverting Configuration**, the input signal is applied to the non-inverting terminal, producing an output that is **in phase** with the input.

The **Voltage Follower** is a special case of the non-inverting amplifier where the feedback resistor is replaced by a direct wire ($R_f = 0$) and the input resistor is open ($R_1 = \infty$), resulting in a unity voltage gain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Volume Knob & Echo" Analogy:** Imagine a public address (PA) system microphone connected to a loudspeaker. If you place the microphone too close to the speaker, the system howls (positive feedback → oscillation). To control this, an audio engineer routes a small portion of the speaker's output back into the system in *opposite phase* (negative feedback) using a **volume knob** (the feedback resistor $R_f$). 
>
> - **Inverting Amplifier** = The engineer flips the speaker polarity to cancel the original sound. The output is an **inverted copy** of the input, scaled by the knob setting. Think of a periscope mirror: what goes up, comes back down.
> - **Non-Inverting Amplifier** = The speaker keeps the original polarity. The output is a **true amplified copy** of the input. Think of a magnifying glass — same orientation, just bigger.
> - **Voltage Follower** = The engineer turns the volume knob to "unity" (no scaling). The signal is reproduced **exactly as it is**, but with the **muscle** of the amplifier behind it. Think of a relay runner who carries the baton at the same speed, but doesn't tire as quickly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need the Voltage Follower?** A sensor (like a thermometer or microphone) often has high internal resistance. If you connect it directly to a low-resistance load, the signal collapses. The voltage follower acts as an **impedance transformer** — it presents a **very high input impedance** (doesn't load the sensor) and a **very low output impedance** (drives the load easily). The signal is preserved perfectly in voltage, but its *driving capability* is massively enhanced.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Op-Amp IC used in KTU Labs:** **µA741** (or equivalent like LM741, TL081)
> - **Ideal Op-Amp Parameters (theoretical benchmarks):**
>   - **Open-loop voltage gain ($A_{OL}$):** $\infty$ (typically $2 \times 10^5$ for 741)
>   - **Input impedance ($Z_{in}$):** $\infty$ (typically $2 \text{ M}\Omega$ for 741)
>   - **Output impedance ($Z_{out}$):** $0$ (typically $75 \text{ }\Omega$ for 741)
>   - **Bandwidth (BW):** $\infty$ (Gain-Bandwidth Product for 741 ≈ **1 MHz**)
>   - **Input offset voltage:** $0$
> - **DC Supply voltages (dual polarity):** **$+V_{CC} = +15\text{ V}$** and **$-V_{EE} = -15\text{ V}$** (standard KTU lab bench power supply)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Two Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amps

Every analysis of an op-amp circuit begins with these two assumptions (valid when the op-amp operates in its **linear region** with negative feedback):

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 1 — Virtual Short:** The voltage difference between the two input terminals is approximately zero. $V_+ \approx V_-$
>
> **Rule 2 — Virtual Open (No input current):** The current into both input terminals is approximately zero. $I_+ \approx I_- \approx 0$

These two rules govern all three configurations we study in this experiment.

## 2.2 Configuration 1: Inverting Amplifier — Step-by-Step Logic

**Circuit Description:** The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting `(-)` terminal through resistor $R_1$. The non-inverting `(+)` terminal is grounded. A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output back to the inverting input.

**Logical Analysis Steps:**
1. By **Rule 2 (Virtual Open)**, no current flows into the `(-)` terminal. Therefore, all current flowing through $R_1$ must flow through $R_f$.
2. By **Rule 1 (Virtual Short)**, since the `(+)` terminal is grounded ($V_+ = 0$), the `(-)` terminal is also at $0$ V. This point is called the **virtual ground**.
3. The current through $R_1$ is: $I = \dfrac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$
4. The same current flows through $R_f$: $I = \dfrac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$
5. Equating the two current expressions yields the closed-loop gain.

**Key Properties:**
- Output is **inverted** (180° phase shift) relative to the input.
- Input impedance $Z_{in} = R_1$ (relatively low).
- Virtual ground makes the circuit **independent of the op-amp's intrinsic gain** — only the external resistors matter.

## 2.3 Configuration 2: Non-Inverting Amplifier — Step-by-Step Logic

**Circuit Description:** The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the non-inverting `(+)` terminal. The inverting `(-)` terminal is connected to a voltage divider formed by $R_1$ (to ground) and $R_f$ (to output).

**Logical Analysis Steps:**
1. By **Rule 1 (Virtual Short)**, the voltage at the `(-)` terminal equals $V_{in}$.
2. The voltage divider at the inverting input gives: $V_- = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$
3. Equating the two: $V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$
4. Rearranging gives the gain expression.

**Key Properties:**
- Output is **in phase** with the input (no inversion).
- Input impedance is **very high** (limited only by op-amp's intrinsic input impedance).
- The voltage gain is always **greater than 1** (cannot attenuate).

## 2.4 Configuration 3: Voltage Follower (Unity Gain Buffer) — Step-by-Step Logic

**Circuit Description:** The output is connected **directly** to the inverting input. The non-inverting input receives the input signal. This is simply the non-inverting amplifier with $R_f = 0$ and $R_1 = \infty$ (open).

**Logical Analysis Steps:**
1. By direct connection: $V_- = V_{out}$.
2. By **Rule 1 (Virtual Short)**: $V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$.
3. Therefore: $V_{out} = V_{in}$ (unity gain).

**Key Properties:**
- **Input impedance:** Extremely high (the op-amp's intrinsic $Z_{in}$, typically $> 10^6 \text{ }\Omega$).
- **Output impedance:** Extremely low (the op-amp's intrinsic $Z_{out}$ divided by loop gain, typically $< 1 \text{ }\Omega$).
- Acts as a perfect **buffer** between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load.

## 2.5 Frequency Response — The Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An op-amp is **not** a perfect amplifier at all frequencies. It has a finite **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)**, which for the µA741 is approximately **1 MHz**. This means as you increase the operating frequency, the closed-loop gain must decrease to keep the product constant.

**Bode Plot Analysis:**
- The frequency response is plotted with frequency on a **logarithmic x-axis** (in Hz) and gain on the y-axis, either in linear scale or **decibels (dB)**.
- The conversion to dB is: $A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$
- At low frequencies, the gain is flat and equal to the designed closed-loop value.
- Beyond a certain **cutoff frequency** $f_H$ (also called $-3$ dB frequency), the gain rolls off at a slope of **$-20$ dB/decade** (or equivalently, $-6$ dB/octave).
- The cutoff frequency for a closed-loop gain $A_{CL}$ is: $f_H = \dfrac{\text{GBW}}{\vert A_{CL} \vert}$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains every formula you need for this experiment. Memorize these — they will appear in both calculations and viva questions.**

| **Configuration** | **Closed-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_v$ or $A_{CL}$)** | **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$)** | **Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$)** | **Phase Relation** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inverting Amplifier** | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $Z_{in} = R_1$ | Very Low (near $0$) | **Inverted** ($-180°$ or $180°$ shift) |
| **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | **Very High** (approaches $\infty$) | Very Low (near $0$) | **In Phase** ($0°$ shift) |
| **Voltage Follower** | $A_v = 1$ (Unity Gain) | **Very High** (maximum possible) | **Very Low** (minimum possible) | **In Phase** ($0°$ shift) |
| **Common to All** | Cutoff Frequency: $f_H = \dfrac{\text{GBW}}{\vert A_{CL} \vert}$ | GBW (741) $\approx 1 \text{ MHz}$ | Roll-off slope: $-20 \text{ dB/decade}$ | dB Conversion: $\text{Gain(dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall in KTU Exams:** Students often confuse the sign convention. Remember:
> - Inverting: gain is **negative** → $180°$ phase shift → **always** shown with a minus sign in the formula.
> - Non-inverting: gain is **positive** and **$\geq 1$** → the formula has the form $(1 + R_f/R_1)$.
> - Voltage follower is a **special case** of the non-inverting amplifier, NOT a separate device.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Inverting Amplifier** is widely used in **active filter design** (low-pass, high-pass), **audio mixers** (summing amplifier is an extension), **D/A converter output stages**, and **phase-shift oscillators**.
> - **Non-Inverting Amplifier** is used in **sensor signal conditioning** (where loading must be avoided), **precision instrumentation amplifiers**, and **voltage references**.
> - **Voltage Follower** is the most common **buffer IC** in industry — used to **isolate** high-impedance sources from low-impedance loads, drive long cables, and feed signals into **A/D converters** that demand low source impedance for accurate sampling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Gain of the Inverting Amplifier

We will derive the gain formula rigorously. Every algebraic step is shown.

**Given Circuit:** Input $V_{in}$ applied through $R_1$ to the inverting terminal `(-)`. The non-inverting terminal `(+)` is grounded. Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output to the inverting input.

**Step 1:** Identify node voltages.

By the ideal op-amp assumption (Rule 1: Virtual Short), and since the non-inverting terminal is grounded:

$$V_+ = 0 \text{ V}$$

$$V_- \approx V_+ = 0 \text{ V}$$

The inverting input is at **0 V** even though it is not physically connected to ground. This is the **virtual ground** concept.

**Step 2:** Identify the currents.

By Rule 2 (Virtual Open), the current into the inverting terminal is zero:

$$i_- = 0 \text{ A}$$

Therefore, by **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the inverting node, the current through $R_1$ must equal the current through $R_f$ (they are in series with no branch escape):

$$i_1 = i_f$$

**Step 3:** Express the current through $R_1$ using Ohm's Law.

The voltage across $R_1$ is $(V_{in} - V_-) = (V_{in} - 0)$:

$$i_1 = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 4:** Express the current through $R_f$ using Ohm's Law.

The voltage across $R_f$ is $(V_- - V_{out}) = (0 - V_{out})$:

$$i_f = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the current equality from Step 2.

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to solve for the gain $A_v = V_{out} / V_{in}$.

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

$$\boxed{A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

**Why is there a negative sign?** The negative sign is a **mathematical signature of the 180° phase inversion**. If $V_{in} = +1$ V, and the gain is $-10$, then $V_{out} = -10$ V. The output is the *negative* (inverted mirror image) of the input.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Loop Gain of the Non-Inverting Amplifier

**Given Circuit:** Input $V_{in}$ applied directly to the non-inverting `(+)` terminal. Resistor $R_1$ connects the inverting `(-)` terminal to ground. Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output back to the inverting terminal.

**Step 1:** Identify node voltages.

$$V_+ = V_{in}$$

By Rule 1 (Virtual Short):

$$V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 2:** Analyze the voltage divider at the inverting input.

The resistors $R_1$ and $R_f$ form a voltage divider between $V_{out}$ and ground. The voltage at the junction (the $V_-$ node) is:

$$V_- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for $V_-$.

$$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the gain $A_v = V_{out} / V_{in}$.

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

$$\boxed{A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

**Verification of limits:**
- If $R_f = 0$ (direct wire) and $R_1 = \infty$ (open), then $A_v = 1 + 0 = 1$. ✓ This confirms the voltage follower result.
- If $R_f = 0$ but $R_1$ is finite, then $A_v = 1 + 0 = 1$ as well — a voltage follower.
- The gain is always $\geq 1$ because both terms in the sum are non-negative. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Voltage Follower Gain

**Step 1:** Direct connection gives $V_- = V_{out}$.

**Step 2:** Virtual short gives $V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$.

**Step 3:** Combining:

$$V_{out} = V_{in}$$

$$\boxed{A_v = 1}$$

**Phase:** Output is **in phase** with input (0° phase shift).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Frequency Response — Gain-Bandwidth Product

**Step 1:** The open-loop gain of a typical op-amp is not constant; it falls with frequency. For the µA741, the open-loop response is characterized by a single-pole transfer function:

$$A_{OL}(f) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j \frac{f}{f_1}}$$

where $A_0$ is the DC open-loop gain (typically $\approx 2 \times 10^5$ for 741) and $f_1$ is the dominant pole frequency (typically $\approx 5$ Hz for 741).

**Step 2:** The Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) is defined as the product of the open-loop gain magnitude and the frequency at which it is measured. For frequencies $f \gg f_1$, the magnitude is approximately:

$$|A_{OL}(f)| \approx \frac{A_0 \cdot f_1}{f}$$

This product $A_0 \cdot f_1$ is a **constant** characteristic of the op-amp:

$$\text{GBW} = A_0 \cdot f_1 \approx 1 \text{ MHz (for µA741)}$$

**Step 3:** For a closed-loop amplifier with gain $A_{CL}$, the closed-loop bandwidth $f_H$ is determined by the point where the closed-loop gain line intersects the open-loop roll-off curve. By the GBW definition:

$$|A_{CL}| \cdot f_H = \text{GBW}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$f_H = \frac{\text{GBW}}{|A_{CL}|}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Design Example (Full KTU Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Task:** Design an inverting amplifier with a closed-loop gain of $-10$. Calculate the expected cutoff frequency. Then verify with a non-inverting amplifier of gain $11$.

**Step 1: Choose $R_1$ for the inverting amplifier.**

A standard starting value is $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$. Then:

$$R_f = |A_v| \cdot R_1 = 10 \times 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Calculate the cutoff frequency.**

$$f_H = \frac{\text{GBW}}{|A_{CL}|} = \frac{1 \text{ MHz}}{10} = 100 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3: Convert gain to dB for the Bode plot.**

$$A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(10) = 20 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4: For the non-inverting amplifier with gain 11.**

Using the same $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$R_f = (A_v - 1) \cdot R_1 = (11 - 1) \times 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Cutoff frequency:

$$f_H = \frac{1 \text{ MHz}}{11} \approx 90.9 \text{ kHz}$$

Gain in dB:

$$A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(11) \approx 20.83 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5: For the voltage follower (gain = 1).**

$$f_H = \frac{1 \text{ MHz}}{1} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

This is the **maximum possible bandwidth** for any configuration built with this op-amp.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| **Parameter** | **Inverting** | **Non-Inverting** | **Voltage Follower** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Example Design (Gain)** | $A_v = -10$ | $A_v = +11$ | $A_v = +1$ |
| **R₁ chosen** | $1 \text{ k}\Omega$ | $1 \text{ k}\Omega$ | $\infty$ (open) |
| **R_f calculated** | $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ | $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ | $0 \text{ }\Omega$ (short) |
| **Gain in dB** | $20 \text{ dB}$ | $20.83 \text{ dB}$ | $0 \text{ dB}$ |
| **$f_H$ (cutoff)** | $100 \text{ kHz}$ | $\approx 90.9 \text{ kHz}$ | $1 \text{ MHz}$ |
| **Phase at output** | $180°$ inverted | $0°$ in phase | $0°$ in phase |
| **Input Impedance** | $1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (low) | Very high ($> 2 \text{ M}\Omega$) | Maximum ($> 2 \text{ M}\Omega$) |
| **Application** | Mixer, filter, phase shifter | Sensor amplifier, instrumentation | Buffer, line driver, ADC driver |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Architecture — Three Configurations (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SUPPLY["DC Power Supply"]
        VCC[+15V]
        VEE[-15V]
    end

    subgraph INVERTING["Inverting Amplifier Block"]
        VS1[Sine Wave Generator] -->|Vin through R1| N1[Node: Inverting Input]
        GND1[Ground] -->|to non-inverting| N2[Node: Non-Inverting]
        N1 -->|feedback Rf| OUT1[Output Node]
        OUT1 -->|Vout inverted| LOAD1[Oscilloscope CH1]
    end

    subgraph NONINVERTING["Non-Inverting Amplifier Block"]
        VS2[Sine Wave Generator] -->|Vin direct| N3[Node: Non-Inverting]
        GND2[Ground] -->|via R1| N4[Node: Inverting Input]
        N3 --> N4
        N4 -->|feedback Rf| OUT2[Output Node]
        OUT2 -->|Vout in phase| LOAD2[Oscilloscope CH1]
    end

    subgraph FOLLOWER["Voltage Follower Block"]
        VS3[Sine Wave Generator] -->|Vin| N5[Node: Non-Inverting]
        N5 --> N6[Node: Inverting Input]
        N6 -->|direct wire feedback| OUT3[Output Node]
        OUT3 -->|Vout equals Vin| LOAD3[Oscilloscope CH1]
    end

    VCC -->|pin 7| OP1[Op-Amp 741]
    VEE -->|pin 4| OP1
```

## 4.2 Pinout & Internal Signal Flow of µA741 Op-Amp

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph IC741["IC 741 - 8 Pin DIP Package"]
        P1[Pin 1: Offset Null]
        P2[Pin 2: Inverting Input -]
        P3[Pin 3: Non-Inverting Input +]
        P4[Pin 4: V- Supply -15V]
        P5[Pin 5: Offset Null]
        P6[Pin 6: Output]
        P7[Pin 7: V+ Supply +15V]
        P8[Pin 8: NC]
    end

    P2 -->|through R1| SIG_IN[Input Signal]
    P6 -->|through Rf| P2
    P3 -->|grounded for inverting| GND
    P6 --> OUTPUT[Output to CRO]
```

## 4.3 Frequency Response — Bode Plot Concept

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LOWF["LOW FREQUENCY REGION"]
        LF1[Flat gain = A_CL in dB]
        LF2[Below fH: gain is constant]
    end

    subgraph MIDF["TRANSITION REGION"]
        TF1[At f = fH: gain drops by 3 dB]
        TF2[From A_CL dB to A_CL minus 3 dB]
    end

    subgraph HIGHF["HIGH FREQUENCY ROLLOFF"]
        HF1[Slope = minus 20 dB per decade]
        HF2[Gain = GBW divided by f]
        HF3[Eventually reaches 0 dB at f = GBW]
    end

    LF1 --> TF1
    TF1 --> HF1
    HF1 --> HF2
    HF2 --> HF3
```

## 4.4 Step-by-Step Lab Connection Flow (Breadboard Wiring)

```mermaid
graph TB
    STEP1[Step 1: Place IC 741 on breadboard] --> STEP2[Step 2: Connect Pin 7 to +15V and Pin 4 to -15V]
    STEP2 --> STEP3[Step 3: Connect Pin 3 non-inverting input to GND for inverting configuration]
    STEP3 --> STEP4[Step 4: Connect R1 between function generator output and Pin 2]
    STEP4 --> STEP5[Step 5: Connect Rf between Pin 6 output and Pin 2]
    STEP5 --> STEP6[Step 6: Connect CRO probes: CH1 to Vin and CH2 to Vout]
    STEP6 --> STEP7[Step 7: Set function generator to 1 kHz sine wave, 1V peak]
    STEP7 --> STEP8[Step 8: Observe CRO waveforms and record amplitude and phase]
    STEP8 --> STEP9[Step 9: Vary frequency from 10 Hz to 1 MHz in decade steps]
    STEP9 --> STEP10[Step 10: Record Vout at each frequency and calculate gain in dB]
    STEP10 --> STEP11[Step 11: Plot Gain dB vs log frequency on semi-log graph paper]
    STEP11 --> STEP12[Step 12: Mark the minus 3 dB point as fH and verify GBW product]
```

## 4.5 Apparatus Required (KTU Lab Record Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> | **S.No** | **Component / Equipment** | **Specification** | **Quantity** |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | 1 | Op-Amp IC | **µA741** (8-pin DIP) | 1 |
> | 2 | Resistor $R_1$ | **1 kΩ**, ¼ W, ±5% | 1 |
> | 3 | Resistor $R_f$ | **10 kΩ**, ¼ W, ±5% | 1 |
> | 4 | DC Power Supply | Dual, **±15 V**, 500 mA | 1 |
> | 5 | Function Generator | **0.1 Hz to 1 MHz**, sine/square/triangle | 1 |
> | 6 | Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) | **20 MHz** or higher, dual trace | 1 |
> | 7 | Breadboard & Connecting Wires | Standard | As required |
> | 8 | Multi-meter | Digital, **3½ digit** | 1 |

## 4.6 Expected Output Waveform Patterns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverting Amplifier:** CRO shows two sine waves of the **same frequency** but **opposite phase** (when one is at peak positive, the other is at peak negative). The Vout peak-to-peak is **$|A_v| = 10$ times** larger than Vin (within bandwidth).

> **Non-Inverting Amplifier:** Two sine waves are **in phase** (peaks aligned). Vout is **11 times** larger than Vin (within bandwidth).

> **Voltage Follower:** Two sine waves are **identical in amplitude and phase** (one completely overlays the other). The benefit is invisible on the CRO until you load the output — then the follower holds the voltage steady while a direct connection would sag.

---
