# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

### **Q1. Define voltage follower. What is its voltage gain and why is it used?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

A **voltage follower** is an op-amp configuration in which the output is connected **directly** to the inverting input, and the input signal is applied to the non-inverting input. It is a special case of the non-inverting amplifier with $R_f = 0$ and $R_1 = \infty$.

The **closed-loop voltage gain** is:

$$A_v = 1$$

It is used as a **buffer amplifier** to provide **impedance matching** between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load. It has **very high input impedance** (does not load the source) and **very low output impedance** (can drive heavy loads). It is commonly used to drive long cables, feed A/D converter inputs, and isolate cascaded amplifier stages.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between the ideal and practical characteristics of an op-amp used in these configurations.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Ideal Op-Amp** | **Practical µA741 Op-Amp** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Open-loop voltage gain ($A_{OL}$) | $\infty$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^5$ |
| Input impedance ($Z_{in}$) | $\infty$ | $\approx 2 \text{ M}\Omega$ |
| Output impedance ($Z_{out}$) | $0$ | $\approx 75 \text{ }\Omega$ |
| Bandwidth | $\infty$ | GBW $\approx 1 \text{ MHz}$ |
| Input offset voltage | $0$ | $\approx 1$ to $6 \text{ mV}$ |

For analysis purposes, we treat the op-amp as ideal, which gives the **two golden rules** (virtual short and virtual open). These simplify the derivations of all three configurations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, you have an **internal choice**. We provide two complete alternative questions below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Inverting and Non-Inverting Amplifier Design**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Design an **inverting amplifier** using a µA741 op-amp to have a closed-loop gain of **$-20$**. Draw the circuit diagram and derive the gain expression. Calculate the expected **$-3$ dB bandwidth** if the op-amp's gain-bandwidth product is $1$ MHz. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **non-inverting amplifier** using the same op-amp to have a closed-loop gain of **$+11$**. Draw the circuit diagram and derive the gain expression. Compare the input impedance of this configuration with the inverting amplifier built in part (a). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Choose $R_1$.** A standard value: $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_f$** using $A_v = -R_f / R_1$:

$$R_f = |A_v| \times R_1 = 20 \times 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the bandwidth:**

$$f_H = \frac{\text{GBW}}{|A_{CL}|} = \frac{1 \text{ MHz}}{20} = 50 \text{ kHz}$$

**Circuit Diagram:** (The student must draw the standard inverting amplifier: $V_{in}$ → $R_1$ → Pin 2, $R_f$ from Pin 6 to Pin 2, Pin 3 grounded, Pins 7 and 4 to ±15V.)

**Derivation of Gain:**

The non-inverting terminal is grounded: $V_+ = 0$.

By virtual short: $V_- = 0$ (virtual ground).

Current through $R_1$:

$$i_1 = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

This same current flows through $R_f$ (virtual open, no current into op-amp input):

$$i_f = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Equating: $V_{in}/R_1 = -V_{out}/R_f$, hence:

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -\frac{20 \text{ k}\Omega}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = -20$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Choose $R_1$.** Same as before: $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_f$** using $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_1$:

$$11 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} \implies R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of Gain:**

By virtual short: $V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$.

Voltage divider at inverting input:

$$V_- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

Equating and rearranging:

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + 10 = 11$$

**Step 4 — Input Impedance Comparison:**

For the **inverting amplifier** (part a), the input impedance seen by the source is:

$$Z_{in} \approx R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

This is relatively **low** because the source drives current through $R_1$ into the virtual ground.

For the **non-inverting amplifier** (part b), the input impedance is the op-amp's intrinsic input impedance itself (since the source drives the non-inverting pin directly):

$$Z_{in} \approx 2 \text{ M}\Omega \text{ (for 741)}$$

This is **vastly higher** than the inverting case. The non-inverting amplifier therefore does **not load** the input signal source, making it ideal for high-impedance sensors.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Voltage Follower and Frequency Response**

> **[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **voltage follower** using a µA741 op-amp. Derive its voltage gain. List **two practical applications** of this configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp has a **gain-bandwidth product of 1 MHz** and is used in an inverting amplifier with $R_1 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 18 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate the **closed-loop gain**, the **$-3$ dB cutoff frequency**, and the **expected gain in dB at 500 kHz**. Sketch the **Bode magnitude plot** qualitatively. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Circuit:** The output pin (Pin 6) is directly connected to the inverting input pin (Pin 2) using a short wire. The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to Pin 3 (non-inverting input). Pins 7 and 4 are connected to +15V and -15V respectively.

**Operation:** By the virtual short concept: $V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$. Since Pin 2 is physically shorted to Pin 6 (the output): $V_- = V_{out}$. Therefore:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \implies A_v = 1$$

**Two Practical Applications:**
1. **Buffer Amplifier:** Used to isolate a high-impedance source (like a piezoelectric sensor or a high-value resistive divider) from a low-impedance load, preventing signal attenuation.
2. **Active Rectifier / Precision Diode Driver:** Used in precision half-wave and full-wave rectifier circuits where the diode forward voltage drop must be eliminated — the op-amp output drives the diode and the feedback ensures accuracy.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Closed-loop gain:**

$$A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -\frac{18 \text{ k}\Omega}{2 \text{ k}\Omega} = -9$$

The magnitude is $|A_v| = 9$.

**Step 2 — Cutoff frequency $f_H$:**

$$f_H = \frac{\text{GBW}}{|A_{CL}|} = \frac{1 \text{ MHz}}{9} \approx 111.1 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Gain in dB at low frequencies (DC to $f_H$):**

$$A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(9) \approx 19.08 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Gain in dB at 500 kHz:**

Since $500 \text{ kHz} > f_H = 111.1 \text{ kHz}$, we are on the roll-off slope. The roll-off is $-20$ dB/decade.

Number of decades above $f_H$:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{500 \text{ kHz}}{111.1 \text{ kHz}}\right) = \log_{10}(4.5) \approx 0.653$$

Attenuation from $f_H$ to 500 kHz:

$$0.653 \times 20 \text{ dB} = 13.06 \text{ dB}$$

Gain at 500 kHz:

$$19.08 \text{ dB} - 13.06 \text{ dB} \approx 6.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Bode Plot Sketch (qualitative description):**

The plot has frequency on a logarithmic x-axis (from 10 Hz to 1 MHz) and gain in dB on the y-axis.
- From 10 Hz to ~111 kHz: **flat horizontal line at 19.08 dB**.
- At 111 kHz: a small "knee" where the gain has dropped to $19.08 - 3 = 16.08$ dB (the $-3$ dB point).
- From 111 kHz onwards: a **straight line sloping downward at $-20$ dB/decade**, crossing 0 dB at the GBW = 1 MHz point.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Lab Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in inverting gain.** Writing $A_v = R_f / R_1$ instead of $A_v = -R_f / R_1$ will cost you the full derivation mark in part (a).
>
> 2. **Not stating the two golden rules** (virtual short, virtual open) at the start of your derivation. Examiners allocate at least 1 mark for explicitly stating these assumptions. Without them, your derivation appears "unjustified."
>
> 3. **Confusing bandwidth and gain-bandwidth product.** The bandwidth $f_H$ depends on the closed-loop gain. The GBW is fixed by the op-amp. Do not write $f_H = \text{GBW}$ — that is only true when $A_{CL} = 1$.
>
> 4. **In frequency response plots, students often plot linear frequency on the x-axis** — this is **wrong**. Frequency response must always be plotted on a **logarithmic x-axis** (semi-log paper) because the roll-off is exponential in nature.
>
> 5. **Practical lab pitfall:** The 741 op-amp has a **slew rate of only 0.5 V/µs**. At high frequencies or large amplitudes, the output will become a **triangle wave** (slew-rate limited) rather than a clean sine wave. Do not mistake this for a circuit fault — it is a fundamental op-amp limitation.
>
> 6. **Always connect the dual power supply** (±15V) BEFORE applying any input signal. Reversing the supply polarity will permanently damage the IC.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before your exam:**
>
> - **Three configurations, three formulas, one op-amp.**
> - Inverting gain: $A_v = -R_f / R_1$ (output is 180° inverted, low input impedance = $R_1$).
> - Non-inverting gain: $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_1$ (output in phase, very high input impedance).
> - Voltage follower: $A_v = 1$ (direct feedback, $R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$).
> - **Two Golden Rules:** Virtual short ($V_+ = V_-$) and Virtual open ($I_+ = I_- = 0$). State them first in every derivation.
> - **Bandwidth:** $f_H = \text{GBW} / |A_{CL}|$. GBW for 741 is **1 MHz**.
> - **Roll-off slope:** $-20$ dB per decade (or $-6$ dB per octave) beyond $f_H$.
> - **dB formula:** $A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$.
> - **Power supply:** Dual ±15V. Pin 7 = +V, Pin 4 = -V.
> - **Lab tip:** Always measure with the CRO on **dual trace mode** (CH1 = input, CH2 = output) to visualize the phase relationship directly.
> - **Most common exam question:** "Design an amplifier of gain X using op-amp 741 and calculate its bandwidth." — practice this template.
> - **Voltage follower purpose:** Impedance buffering, not amplification. Use it whenever a high-Z source must drive a low-Z load.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and implement Integrator, Differentiator, Schmitt trigger (UTP = +3V and LTP = -3V) using IC 741. Verify the output waveform on CRO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL407 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and implement Integrator, Differentiator, Schmitt trigger (UTP = +3V and LTP = -3V) using IC 741. Verify the output waveform on CRO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 IC 741 Operational Amplifier — The Heart of the Experiment

The **IC 741** is a general-purpose, internally compensated, high-gain **operational amplifier (Op-Amp)** fabricated on a single silicon chip. It consists of a differential amplifier input stage, a high-gain intermediate stage, and a push-pull output stage. The device is housed in an **8-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)** and is the universal workhorse of undergraduate analog electronics laboratories.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The IC 741 is the *de facto* building block for all linear applications in this lab. Its three terminal roles are: **Inverting Input (Pin 2)**, **Non-Inverting Input (Pin 3)**, and **Output (Pin 6)**. Power is supplied through **Pin 7 (+V_CC)** and **Pin 4 (−V_EE)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of the op-amp as a "decision-making seesaw." It continuously compares the voltages at its two input pins. Whatever tiny difference exists (in microvolts) is amplified by an enormous internal gain (typically **2 × 10⁵ V/V**) to produce a substantial output. The surrounding resistors and capacitors — like the people sitting on the seesaw — *steer* this amplification to perform a specific mathematical operation.

### 1.2 The Integrator — "Op-Amp as an Accumulator"

**Formal Definition:** An op-amp integrator is a closed-loop amplifier circuit that produces an output voltage waveform which is the **time-integral of the input signal**, scaled by a constant. The mathematical relationship is:

$$v_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int v_{in}(t) \, dt$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** The negative sign in the integrator equation arises because the input is applied at the **inverting terminal (Pin 2)**. The output is therefore the *inverted integral* of the input.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine pouring water into a bucket at a rate controlled by the input voltage. The water level (output) keeps rising as long as you keep pouring (positive input). If you pour at a constant rate (DC input), the water rises linearly with time. If you alternate pouring and removing (square wave input), the water level forms a **triangular wave** — this is exactly what the integrator does to a square wave!

### 1.3 The Differentiator — "Op-Amp as a Rate Detector"

**Formal Definition:** An op-amp differentiator produces an output voltage proportional to the **time-derivative (rate of change)** of the input signal:

$$v_{out}(t) = -RC \, \frac{d v_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a speedometer in a car. The speedometer doesn't tell you *where* the car is (that would be integration); it tells you *how fast* the position is changing (differentiation). If the input is a slowly changing (ramp) signal, the output is a constant. If the input is a triangular wave (linearly rising and falling), the output is a **square wave** — because the slope abruptly changes sign!

### 1.4 The Schmitt Trigger — "Op-Amp with a Hysteresis Memory"

**Formal Definition:** A Schmitt trigger is a **comparator with positive feedback** (regenerative feedback) that converts a slowly varying input signal into a sharply defined two-state output. It exhibits **hysteresis**, meaning it has two distinct threshold voltages:
- **Upper Threshold Voltage (UTP)** — the input level at which the output switches from LOW to HIGH.
- **Lower Threshold Voltage (LTP)** — the input level at which the output switches from HIGH to LOW.

For an **inverting Schmitt trigger** (input applied at Pin 2):

$$V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat} \quad ; \quad V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}$$

where $V_{sat}$ is the saturation voltage of the op-amp output (typically **±13.5 V** for IC 741 with ±15 V supply).

**Intuitive Analogy:** A Schmitt trigger behaves like a **bistable thermostat**. It does not turn ON the heater at, say, 24.9 °C and OFF at 25.0 °C. Instead, it turns ON at 23 °C and OFF at 27 °C. This **dead-band (hysteresis)** prevents the system from rapidly chattering ON/OFF near a single threshold — it provides **noise immunity** to any analog signal fed to a digital system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Schmitt Trigger is Required in this Lab:** The UTP (+3 V) and LTP (−3 V) values specify a hysteresis band of **6 V** centered on zero. This makes the circuit useful for squaring up sinusoidal or triangular signals into clean square waves with a well-defined TTL-compatible logic swing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions (Required to Derive the Equations)

To analyze all three circuits, the op-amp is treated as *ideal* with the following consequences:

1. **Infinite open-loop gain (A_OL → ∞):** The differential input voltage $v_d = v^+ - v^-$ is effectively **zero**. Therefore, the inverting and non-inverting terminal voltages are virtually equal: $v^+ = v^-$ (the **virtual short** concept).
2. **Infinite input impedance (Z_in → ∞):** **No current flows into either input terminal**: $i^+ = i^- = 0$.
3. **Zero output impedance (Z_out = 0):** The output voltage is independent of the load.
4. **Infinite bandwidth and zero offset.**

### 2.2 Deep Dive: Op-Amp Integrator (Theoretical)

**Step 1 — Node Equation at Inverting Terminal:** Since no current enters the op-amp (assumption 2), all current flowing through the input resistor $R$ must flow through the feedback capacitor $C$.

$$i_R = i_C$$

**Step 2 — Express Each Current Using Capacitor and Resistor Laws:**

$$i_R = \frac{v_{in} - v^-}{R} = \frac{v_{in} - 0}{R} = \frac{v_{in}}{R}$$

This uses the **virtual ground** concept: $v^- = v^+ = 0$ V (since the non-inverting input is grounded).

**Step 3 — Capacitor Current Relation:** The current into a capacitor is $i_C = C \cdot \frac{dv_C}{dt}$. For the feedback capacitor, the voltage across it is $v_C = v^- - v_{out} = 0 - v_{out} = -v_{out}$. Therefore:

$$i_C = C \cdot \frac{d(-v_{out})}{dt} = -C \cdot \frac{dv_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Rearrange:**

$$\frac{v_{in}}{R} = -C \cdot \frac{dv_{out}}{dt}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dv_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{1}{RC} \cdot v_{in}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Both Sides:**

$$v_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int v_{in}(t) \, dt + v_{out}(0)$$

The constant $v_{out}(0)$ is the initial condition (the capacitor's stored voltage at $t = 0$).

### 2.3 Deep Dive: Op-Amp Differentiator (Theoretical)

**Step 1 — Apply the Virtual Short and Infinite Impedance:** Same as before, $v^- = 0$ and the input current equals the feedback current.

**Step 2 — Input Branch Current Through C:**

$$i_C = C \cdot \frac{d(v_{in} - v^-)}{dt} = C \cdot \frac{dv_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Feedback Branch Current Through R:**

$$i_R = \frac{v^- - v_{out}}{R} = \frac{0 - v_{out}}{R} = -\frac{v_{out}}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Equate $i_C = i_R$:**

$$C \cdot \frac{dv_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{v_{out}}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v_{out}$:**

$$v_{out}(t) = -RC \cdot \frac{dv_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

### 2.4 Deep Dive: Inverting Schmitt Trigger (Theoretical)

The non-inverting terminal is connected to a voltage divider formed by $R_1$ (from output to Pin 3) and $R_2$ (from Pin 3 to ground). By superposition (or simply the voltage divider rule), the voltage at the non-inverting input is:

$$v^+ = v_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 1 — Switching Condition:** The op-amp output saturates at $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ depending on whether $v_{in} > v^+$ or $v_{in} < v^+$.

**Step 2 — Upper Threshold (UTP):** When $v_{out} = +V_{sat}$, the threshold is:

$$V_{UTP} = +V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Lower Threshold (LTP):** When $v_{out} = -V_{sat}$, the threshold is:

$$V_{LTP} = -V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis Width:**

$$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 2V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Integrator Output | $v_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}\,dt$ | Time-integral of input | V |
| Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | Sets the integration speed | s |
| Differentiator Output | $v_{out} = -RC\,\frac{dv_{in}}{dt}$ | Time-derivative of input | V |
| Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | Sets the differentiation speed | s |
| Feedback Factor | $\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Fraction of output fed back | dimensionless |
| UTP | $V_{UTP} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Upper switching threshold | V |
| LTP | $V_{LTP} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Lower switching threshold | V |
| Hysteresis Width | $V_H = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Total noise-immunity band | V |
| Threshold Ratio | $\frac{V_{UTP}}{V_{sat}} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Design equation for resistors | dimensionless |
| Slew Rate (IC 741) | $SR = 0.5$ | Limits max frequency of operation | V / μs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule for KTU Lab:** A square wave of **1 kHz, ±5 V peak** applied to an integrator with **R = 10 kΩ** and **C = 0.01 μF** produces a triangular wave of amplitude ≈ 8 V (limited by op-amp saturation). Always use $\tau = RC \gg T/2$ (where $T$ is the input period) for proper integration.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

* **Integrator** forms the core of **analog computers, ramp generators, ADC architectures (dual-slope)**, and is the basis of every **active filter** (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass) used in audio and instrumentation.
* **Differentiator** is used in **PID controllers (the D-term)**, **edge detectors in radar/sonar**, and **FM demodulators**.
* **Schmitt Trigger** is the fundamental input stage of every **logic gate** (TTL/CMOS), **function generator** (square wave shaper), and **zero-crossing detector** in power electronics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Integrator Output for a Square-Wave Input

**Problem Setup:** A square wave of amplitude $V_m$ and period $T$ is applied to an ideal integrator. Derive the peak-to-peak output amplitude.

**Step 1:** For a square wave, $v_{in} = +V_m$ during the first half-period (from $t = 0$ to $t = T/2$) and $v_{in} = -V_m$ during the second half.

**Step 2:** For the positive half-cycle ($v_{in} = +V_m$):

$$v_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^{T/2} (+V_m) \, dt = -\frac{V_m}{RC} \cdot \frac{T}{2}$$

**Step 3:** The output ramps down linearly from $0$ to $-\frac{V_m T}{2RC}$. Define the peak-to-peak swing across one full period:

$$V_{pp,out} = 2 \cdot \frac{V_m T}{2RC} = \frac{V_m T}{RC} = \frac{V_m}{RCf}$$

where $f = 1/T$ is the input frequency.

**Step 4:** Substitute $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$, $V_m = 5$ V, $f = 1$ kHz:

$$V_{pp,out} = \frac{5}{(10 \times 10^3)(0.01 \times 10^{-6})(1 \times 10^3)} = \frac{5}{0.1} = 50 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Caveat:** The result (50 V) is impossible — the op-amp saturates at ±13.5 V! This is why in practice we use a **slower time constant** or **smaller input amplitude**. A real lab setup uses $R = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$ for a clean triangular wave of ~5 V peak.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differentiator Output for a Triangular-Wave Input

**Problem Setup:** A triangular wave of peak amplitude $V_m$ and period $T$ is applied to a differentiator with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$. Find the output.

**Step 1:** The slope of a symmetric triangular wave is:

$$\frac{dv_{in}}{dt} = +\frac{2V_m}{T/2} = +\frac{4V_m}{T}$$

during the rising portion (positive slope) and $-\frac{4V_m}{T}$ during the falling portion.

**Step 2:** Apply the differentiator equation:

$$v_{out} = -RC \cdot \frac{dv_{in}}{dt} = -RC \cdot \left(\frac{4V_m}{T}\right) = -\frac{4RC \cdot V_m}{T}$$

**Step 3:** For the **falling** portion, the slope is negative, so the output is **positive** square pulse. The output is therefore a **square wave** of amplitude:

$$V_{out,peak} = \frac{4RC \cdot V_m}{T} = 4RC \cdot V_m \cdot f$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $R = 10^4 \, \Omega$, $C = 10^{-8} \, F$, $V_m = 2$ V, $f = 1$ kHz:

$$V_{out,peak} = 4 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2 \times 10^3 = 4 \times 0.0001 \times 2 \times 1000$$

$$V_{out,peak} = 0.8 \text{ V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is small:** A pure differentiator has a gain that rises with frequency (6 dB/octave), making it **unstable and noise-sensitive**. The **practical differentiator** adds a small series resistor $R_1$ and feedback capacitor $C_1$ to limit high-frequency gain and prevent oscillation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Design of Schmitt Trigger Resistors for UTP = +3 V, LTP = −3 V

**Design Specifications Given:**
- $V_{UTP} = +3$ V
- $V_{LTP} = -3$ V
- IC 741 powered by $\pm 15$ V, so $V_{sat} \approx 13.5$ V (typical, not 15 V)
- Choose a standard value for $R_2$ and solve for $R_1$.

**Step 1:** Write the UTP equation:

$$V_{UTP} = +V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = +13.5 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 3$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the resistor ratio:

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{3}{13.5} = 0.2222$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply:

$$R_2 = 0.2222(R_1 + R_2) \implies R_2 - 0.2222 R_2 = 0.2222 R_1$$

$$0.7778 R_2 = 0.2222 R_1 \implies R_1 = \frac{0.7778}{0.2222} R_2 \approx 3.5 R_2$$

**Step 4:** Choose a standard value for $R_2$. The standard E12 series nearest to the calculated ratio is $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 5:** Compute $R_1$:

$$R_1 = 3.5 \times 10 = 35 \text{ k}\Omega$$

But this is **not a standard value**. Use the nearest E12 standard: $R_1 = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$ (gives ratio 0.232, UTP = 3.14 V — close enough) **OR** $R_1 = 39 \text{ k}\Omega$ (ratio 0.204, UTP = 2.75 V).

> [!NOTE]
> **Preferred KTU Standard Component Selection:** Most KTU lab manuals use $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, which gives:
> $$\beta = \frac{2.2}{12.2} = 0.180$$
> $$V_{UTP} = 0.180 \times 13.5 = 2.43 \text{ V}$$
> This is slightly less than +3 V, but with $\pm 12$ V supplies (yielding $V_{sat} \approx 10$ V), we get $V_{UTP} \approx 1.8$ V. Hence, $R_1$ must be tuned using a preset (potentiometer) in the lab.

**Step 6 (Practical Design):** Use a **10 kΩ preset** (potentiometer) for $R_1$ with $R_2 = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$ fixed. Adjust the preset on the breadboard until the CRO displays the **exact ±3 V threshold transitions** for a 1 kHz sine wave input.

### 3.4 Design Summary Table for the Three Sub-Circuits

| **Sub-Circuit** | **Components Used** | **Standard Values** | **Input Signal** | **Expected Output** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Integrator | R, C, IC 741 | R = 10 kΩ, C = 0.01 μF | Square wave 1 kHz, ±5 V | Triangular wave ±5 V |
| Differentiator | R, C, IC 741 | R = 10 kΩ, C = 0.01 μF | Triangular wave 1 kHz, ±2 V | Square wave ±0.8 V |
| Schmitt Trigger | R₁, R₂, IC 741 | R₁ = 10 kΩ (preset), R₂ = 2.2 kΩ | Sine wave 1 kHz, ±10 V | Square wave ±13.5 V |

### 3.5 Component Selection & Power Supply

- **IC 741 Power:** Connect **Pin 7** to **+15 V** and **Pin 4** to **−15 V** from a regulated DC power supply.
- **Decoupling Capacitors:** Place a **0.1 μF ceramic capacitor** between each supply pin and ground to suppress high-frequency noise.
- **Offset Nulling (Optional):** Connect a **10 kΩ potentiometer** between Pins 1, 5, and −15 V to null the output offset voltage.
- **Signal Generator:** A **function generator** capable of producing sine, square, and triangular waves from 1 Hz to 1 MHz with amplitude 0–10 V p-p.
- **CRO:** A **20 MHz dual-channel oscilloscope** with calibrated probes (10× attenuation recommended to minimize loading).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
>
> **Q1. Define an op-amp integrator and write its output equation. Mention the role of the feedback capacitor.**
>
> **Model Answer:** An op-amp integrator is a closed-loop circuit using an op-amp, an input resistor **R**, and a feedback capacitor **C**. The output voltage is the time-integral of the input, scaled by a factor of $-\frac{1}{RC}$: $v_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}\,dt$. The feedback capacitor **C stores charge proportional to the integral of the input current** that flows through R, and the voltage across it (inverted) appears at the output. The negative sign indicates the inverting configuration (input at Pin 2).
>
> ---
>
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
>
> **Q2. What is a Schmitt trigger? Define UTP and LTP. Why is hysteresis important in practical circuits?**
>
> **Model Answer:** A Schmitt trigger is a comparator with **positive (regenerative) feedback** that produces a two-state output. The **UTP (Upper Threshold Point)** is the input voltage at which the output switches from LOW to HIGH, while the **LTP (Lower Threshold Point)** is the input voltage at which the output switches from HIGH to LOW. For an inverting Schmitt trigger, $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$ and $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$, where $\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$. **Hysteresis is important because it provides noise immunity** — small fluctuations in the input near a single threshold do not cause unwanted rapid switching (chattering) of the output, ensuring a clean, stable square wave even for noisy or slowly varying inputs.

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Internal Choice Pattern]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** Design an op-amp integrator using IC 741 to integrate a 1 kHz square wave of amplitude ±5 V. Choose appropriate values of R and C, derive the output expression, and sketch the expected input and output waveforms. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an op-amp differentiator. How does a practical differentiator differ from an ideal one? Mention the two modifications made and justify each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
For integration of a 1 kHz square wave, choose the time constant $\tau = RC$ such that $\tau \gg T/2 = 0.5$ ms but small enough to avoid saturation.
- Choose $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$ → $\tau = 0.1$ ms.
- For $v_{in} = +5$ V (half-cycle of period $T/2 = 0.5$ ms), the output ramps down:
  $$v_{out}(T/2) = -\frac{V_m T}{2RC} = -\frac{5 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^4 \times 10^{-8}} = -2.5 \text{ V}$$
- The output is a **triangular wave of amplitude 2.5 V, 180° out of phase** with the input square wave.
- Circuit: Input → R → Pin 2; C between Pin 2 and Pin 6; Pin 3 grounded; ±15 V supply to Pins 7 and 4.

**Model Solution (b):**
- An op-amp differentiator uses **C at the input branch** and **R in the feedback path**. Applying KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground), $C\frac{dv_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{v_{out}}{R}$, giving $v_{out} = -RC\frac{dv_{in}}{dt}$.
- **Limitations of Ideal Differentiator:** (i) Gain increases linearly with frequency (6 dB/octave), amplifying high-frequency noise. (ii) The input capacitor causes instability due to phase shift at high frequencies. (iii) Input impedance decreases with frequency, loading the source.
- **Practical Differentiator Modifications:** Add a **small input resistor $R_1$ in series with C** to limit high-frequency gain (forms a high-frequency pole with C). Add a **small feedback capacitor $C_1$ in parallel with R** to introduce a high-frequency zero that compensates the pole, ensuring stability. The component values must satisfy $R_1 C = R C_1$ for the high-frequency roll-off to be at the right place.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Pattern, Module 2]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** Design an **inverting Schmitt trigger** using IC 741 with **UTP = +3 V** and **LTP = −3 V**. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5$ V. Determine the resistor values and draw the transfer characteristic. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an op-amp integrator. Derive the output expression starting from KCL. Why is the output of a practical integrator prone to drift, and how is this corrected? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- UTP equation: $V_{UTP} = +V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = 3$ V.
- Substituting $V_{sat} = 13.5$ V: $\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{3}{13.5} = \frac{2}{9} = 0.2222$.
- Cross-multiplying: $9R_2 = 2R_1 + 2R_2 \Rightarrow 2R_1 = 7R_2 \Rightarrow R_1 = 3.5 R_2$.
- Choosing $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard), we get $R_1 = 35 \text{ k}\Omega$. Since this is non-standard, use $R_1 = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$ or a **10 kΩ preset** to fine-tune.
- **Transfer Characteristic:** A rectangular hysteresis loop with horizontal axis = $v_{in}$ and vertical axis = $v_{out}$. The output jumps to $+V_{sat}$ when $v_{in}$ falls below LTP ($-3$ V) and jumps to $-V_{sat}$ when $v_{in}$ rises above UTP ($+3$ V).

**Model Solution (b):**
- The op-amp integrator has **R in the input branch** and **C in the feedback path**. KCL at the inverting node (Pin 2, at virtual ground): $\frac{v_{in} - 0}{R} = C\frac{d(0 - v_{out})}{dt}$.
- Rearranging and integrating: $v_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}(t)\,dt + v_{out}(0)$.
- **Drift in Practical Integrator:** Due to the op-amp's finite **input bias current, input offset voltage**, and **leakage current of C**, the capacitor accumulates a slow DC error that drives the output to saturation over time, even with no input.
- **Correction (Practical Integrator):** Add a **large feedback resistor $R_f$ (typically 10× to 100× R) in parallel with C**. This provides a DC path for the bias current, limiting the low-frequency gain to a finite value $R_f/R$, and prevents saturation. The transfer function becomes: $v_{out}/v_{in} = -R_f / [R(1 + j\omega R_f C)]$, which acts as an integrator only above the corner frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi R_f C)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** Many students write $v_{out} = +\frac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}\,dt$ (without the negative sign) and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong $V_{sat}$ Used:** Students often use $V_{sat} = 15$ V (the supply voltage) instead of the actual measured ≈ 13.5 V, leading to wrong resistor values.
> 3. **Confusing Inverting and Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger:** The given UTP/LTP formulas apply to the **inverting** configuration (input at Pin 2). For non-inverting (input at Pin 3), the formula is $V_{UTP} = +V_{sat}(1 + R_2/R_1) \cdot R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ — a totally different expression! Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Forgetting the Initial Condition:** The full integrator equation includes $v_{out}(0)$, which represents the capacitor's stored voltage. Examiners may deduct marks if this is omitted.
> 5. **Sketching Waveforms Without Axes/Labels:** Always label the time axis (t), the amplitude axis (v), mark the peak values, and show the phase relationship between input and output.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **IC 741 Pinout:** Pin 2 = Inverting, Pin 3 = Non-Inverting, Pin 6 = Output, Pin 4 = V−, Pin 7 = V+, Pin 8 = NC.
- **Virtual Short & Virtual Ground:** Two foundational assumptions. $v^+ = v^-$ and no current flows into the inputs.
- **Integrator Output Equation:** $v_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}\,dt$. Component placement: **R at input, C in feedback**.
- **Differentiator Output Equation:** $v_{out} = -RC\,\frac{dv_{in}}{dt}$. Component placement: **C at input, R in feedback**.
- **Schmitt Trigger UTP/LTP:** $V_{UTP} = +V_{sat} \cdot R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ and $V_{LTP} = -V_{sat} \cdot R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ for the **inverting** configuration.
- **Hysteresis Width:** $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$. Provides noise immunity.
- **$V_{sat}$ for IC 741:** Use **±13.5 V** with ±15 V supply (not ±15 V directly).
- **Square Wave → Integrator → Triangular Wave** (180° phase shift, negative slope for positive input).
- **Triangular Wave → Differentiator → Square Wave** (positive pulses for negative slope, negative pulses for positive slope).
- **Sine Wave → Schmitt Trigger → Square Wave** (clean sharp transitions, square wave amplitude = $\pm V_{sat}$).
- **Practical Integrator Modification:** Add $R_f$ in parallel with C (10×R to 100×R) to prevent DC drift and saturation.
- **Practical Differentiator Modification:** Add $R_1$ in series with C and $C_1$ in parallel with R to limit high-frequency gain and prevent oscillation.
- **CRO Verification Procedure:** Set function generator amplitude to 5 V p-p, frequency 1 kHz. Connect CH-1 of CRO to op-amp output (Pin 6) and CH-2 to function generator output. Observe the phase shift and waveform shape. For the Schmitt trigger, switch the CRO to **XY mode** to view the hysteresis loop directly.
- **Decoupling Capacitors:** Always use 0.1 μF between supply pins and ground to suppress oscillations.
- **Common Component Values for this Lab:** $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$, $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ (preset), $R_2 = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, ±15 V dual supply.
- **Safety Precautions:** Never connect the power supply before verifying the circuit wiring. Always use current-limited supplies to protect the IC from shorts. Switch off the supply before changing any component.
- **Expected CRO Reading Summary:** Integrator (1 kHz square, ±5 V in) → triangular ±2.5 V out, 180° phase shift. Differentiator (1 kHz triangular, ±2 V in) → square ±0.8 V out, in phase with the slope. Schmitt Trigger (1 kHz sine, ±10 V in) → square ±13.5 V out with UTP/LTP at ±3 V.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and implement RC phase shift oscillator/Wein bridge oscillator, Triangular/Saw tooth wave generator, Astable/Monostable multivibrator using IC 741
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRL407 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design and implement RC phase shift oscillator/Wein bridge oscillator, Triangular/Saw tooth wave generator, Astable/Monostable multivibrator using IC 741 # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — Core Definitions & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What Are Signal Generators & Multivibrators? In analog electronics, an **oscillator** is a circuit that produces a periodic, oscillating electronic signal—often a sine wave, square wave, or triangular wave—without any external input signal. A **multivibrator** is a special type of regenerative switching circuit that uses positive feedback to produce square or rectangular waveforms. In KTU 2024 Scheme lab curriculum, the **IC 741 (µA741)** op-amp is used as the active building block for all five sub-experiments in this module. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The IC 741 is an 8-pin operational amplifier with an open-loop gain of approximately **200,000 (2 × 10⁵)**, an input impedance of about **2 MΩ**, and a slew rate of **0.5 V/µs**. It requires ±9 V to ±18 V dual supply (standard: **±15 V**). --- ## 1.2 The Five Sub-Experiments — At a Glance | # | Experiment | Output Waveform | Typical Frequency Range | |---|------------|-----------------|-------------------------| | 1 | **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** | Sine wave (low frequency) | 1 Hz – 100 kHz | | 2 | **Wein Bridge Oscillator** | Sine wave (low distortion) | 1 Hz – 1 MHz | | 3 | **Triangular Wave Generator** | Triangular wave | 10 Hz – 100 kHz | | 4 | **Sawtooth Wave Generator** | Ramp/sawtooth wave | 1 Hz – 100 kHz | | 5 | **Astable/Monostable Multivibrator** | Square/pulse wave | 0.1 Hz – 100 kHz | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swing Push" Idea Imagine a child on a swing: - **Without a push** → swing gradually stops (energy loss = damping) - **With a perfectly timed push** → swing continues forever (sustained oscillation) In an electronic oscillator: - **RC networks** act like the **timing mechanism** of the swing - **Op-amp (IC 741)** provides the **energy push** to compensate for losses - **Barkhausen Criterion** ensures the push is **exactly right** (gain condition + phase condition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Barkhausen Criterion for Sustained Oscillation:** > 1. The loop gain magnitude must equal **unity** ($\vert A\beta \vert = 1$) > 2. The total phase shift around the loop must equal **0° or 360°** ($\angle A\beta = 0°$ or $360°$) --- ## 1.4 The Barkhausen Criterion — Formal Definition For any feedback oscillator, sustained oscillations occur when the product of the amplifier gain $A$ and the feedback factor $\beta$ satisfies both conditions: $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$ $$\angle A\beta = 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$ The first condition ensures the amplifier compensates for all losses, while the second ensures positive feedback. --- ## 1.5 IC 741 — The Workhorse Active Component > [!IMPORTANT] > **IC 741 Pin Configuration (8-Pin DIP):** > - Pin 1 → Offset Null > - Pin 2 → Inverting Input (–) > - Pin 3 → Non-inverting Input (+) > - Pin 4 → V⁻ (Negative supply, –15 V) > - Pin 5 → Offset Null > - Pin 6 → Output > - Pin 7 → V⁺ (Positive supply, +15 V) > - Pin 8 → NC (No Connection) For all experiments in this module, the IC 741 operates in **closed-loop (linear) configuration** for oscillators and in **saturated (switching) configuration** for multivibrators. # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Experiment A — RC Phase Shift Oscillator ### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology The RC phase shift oscillator uses a **single inverting amplifier** (gain = –29) with a **three-stage RC ladder network** in the feedback path. Each RC section provides **60° phase shift**, and the amplifier provides another **180°**, totaling **360° (i.e., 0°)** — satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition. ### 2.1.2 Operational Logic The amplifier must provide a minimum gain of **–29** to overcome the attenuation ($\beta = 1/29$) introduced by the RC ladder. The three identical RC sections (each with $R$ and $C$) produce a total phase shift of $3 \times 60° = 180°$. Combined with the inverting amplifier's 180° shift, the loop phase shift is 360°. ### 2.1.3 Frequency of Oscillation — Derivation Approach The transfer function of the three-section RC ladder is: $$\beta = \frac{1}{29}$$ This occurs only when each section provides exactly 60° shift, which happens at one specific frequency. $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}}$$ ### 2.1.4 Amplifier Gain Requirement For sustained oscillations, the magnitude condition requires: $$\vert A \vert = \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29$$ In the inverting op-amp configuration: $$A = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -29 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 29 R_1$$ --- ## 2.2 Experiment B — Wien Bridge Oscillator ### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology The Wien bridge oscillator uses a **non-inverting amplifier** with a **Wien bridge network** (series RC and parallel RC) providing positive feedback, and a **voltage divider** providing negative feedback. ### 2.2.2 Positive Feedback Network (Wien Network) The Wien network consists of: - **Series arm:** $R$ and $C$ in series - **Parallel arm:** $R$ and $C$ in parallel At the balance frequency, both RC sections produce **zero net phase shift**. ### 2.2.3 Frequency of Oscillation At resonance (zero phase shift), the feedback factor is: $$\beta = \frac{1}{3}$$ The magnitude condition then demands $A \geq 3$. $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ ### 2.2.4 Gain Requirement For a non-inverting amplifier: $$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2 R_1$$ > [!NOTE] > In practice, $R_f$ is implemented using a **non-linear element** (e.g., thermistor or JFET) to provide **automatic gain control (AGC)**, preventing distortion from over-amplification. --- ## 2.3 Experiment C — Triangular Wave Generator ### 2.3.1 Circuit Topology A triangular wave generator consists of: 1. A **square wave generator** (Schmitt trigger using op-amp) 2. An **integrator** (using op-amp) The Schmitt trigger generates a square wave, which is integrated to produce a triangular wave. ### 2.3.2 Frequency of Oscillation For symmetric output (equal positive and negative peaks), the frequency is: $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{4RC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}}$$ Where: - $R_1$ and $R_2$ form the positive feedback divider (Schmitt) - $R$ and $C$ form the integrator timing components The Schmitt trigger thresholds are at $\pm V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_2}$. --- ## 2.4 Experiment D — Sawtooth Wave Generator ### 2.4.1 Circuit Topology A sawtooth generator uses an op-amp with: - A **constant current source** charging a capacitor - A **Schmitt trigger** to discharge the capacitor rapidly when threshold is reached ### 2.4.2 Frequency of Oscillation $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{V_{th}}{V_{sat} \cdot R \cdot C}}$$ For symmetric thresholds: $$f = \frac{1}{2RC}$$ --- ## 2.5 Experiment E — Astable Multivibrator (using IC 741) ### 2.5.1 Circuit Topology The astable multivibrator uses an IC 741 op-amp with: - A **resistive voltage divider** ($R_1$, $R_2$) for positive feedback - An **RC timing circuit** ($R$, $C$) at the inverting input The op-amp output saturates alternately between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$, producing a **square wave**. ### 2.5.2 Frequency of Oscillation $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2RC \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_2}{R_1}\right)}}$$ When $R_1 = R_2 = R$: $$f = \frac{1}{2RC \ln 3} \approx \frac{1}{2.2 RC}$$ --- ## 2.6 Experiment F — Monostable Multivibrator (using IC 741) ### 2.6.1 Circuit Topology The monostable has **one stable state** and **one quasi-stable state**. A trigger pulse forces it to switch to the quasi-stable state for a duration $T$, after which it returns to stable state. ### 2.6.2 Pulse Width $$\boxed{T = RC \ln\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)}$$ When $R_1 = R_2 = R$: $$T = RC \ln 2 \approx 0.693 RC$$ --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers 80% of KTU numerical questions on this topic.** | Circuit | Output Waveform | Frequency / Time Period | Critical Condition | |---------|----------------|-------------------------|--------------------| | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Sine wave | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $A \geq 29$, three 60° RC sections | | Wien Bridge Oscillator | Sine wave | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $A \geq 3$, $\beta = 1/3$ | | Triangular Wave Generator | Triangular wave | $f = \dfrac{R_2}{4 R_1 RC}$ | Square wave → integrator | | Sawtooth Wave Generator | Ramp wave | $f = \dfrac{1}{2RC}$ (symmetric) | Constant current charging | | Astable Multivibrator (741) | Square wave | $f = \dfrac{1}{2RC \ln(1 + 2R_2/R_1)}$ | $R_1, R_2$ set threshold | | Monostable Multivibrator (741) | Single pulse | $T = RC \ln(1 + R_1/R_2)$ | Triggered by external pulse | | Schmitt Trigger (741) | Square wave | — | Hysteresis: $V_H = \dfrac{V_{sat} \cdot R_1}{R_1+R_2}$ | --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications > [!NOTE] > **Where these circuits are used in industry:** > - **Audio signal generators** (test & measurement) → Wien bridge oscillator > - **Function generators** (lab equipment) → Triangular + sine shaper > - **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) time bases** → Sawtooth generator > - **Digital logic clocks** → Astable multivibrator (older designs) > - **Pulse generation for digital circuits** → Monostable multivibrator > - **Missing pulse detectors** → Monostable multivibrator > - **Tone generation in toys & alarms** → All five circuits # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Design Procedures ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RC Phase Shift Oscillator Frequency ### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Feedback Network Consider a single RC high-pass section. Its transfer function is: $$H(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$ This gives a phase shift between 0° and 90° depending on frequency. For the **three-section ladder**, with each section having the same $R$ and $C$, the overall feedback factor is: $$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{(j\omega RC)^3}{1 + 5(j\omega RC) - 6(j\omega RC)^2 + (j\omega RC)^3}$$ This is derived by cascade multiplication of three identical RC section transfer functions, accounting for loading effects between stages. ### 3.1.2 Solving for 180° Phase Shift For oscillation, we require $\angle \beta = 180°$. Substituting $s = j\omega$ and separating real and imaginary parts: $$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{-j\omega^3 R^3 C^3}{1 - 5\omega^2 R^2 C^2 + j\omega RC(5 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2)}$$ For the denominator to produce 180° phase, its imaginary part must equal zero contribution, which simplifies to the oscillation condition. The detailed algebra yields: $$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 6$$ ### 3.1.3 Final Frequency Expression Taking the square root: $$\omega RC = \sqrt{6}$$ Since $\omega = 2\pi f$: $$2\pi f \cdot RC = \sqrt{6}$$ Solving for $f$: $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}}$$ ### 3.1.4 Feedback Factor at Oscillation Frequency At $\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 6$, the feedback factor magnitude is: $$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}$$ Therefore, the amplifier must satisfy: $$\vert A \vert = 29 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 29 R_1$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wien Bridge Oscillator Frequency ### 3.2.1 Wien Network Transfer Function The Wien network has two arms: - **Series arm:** $Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ - **Parallel arm:** $Z_p = \dfrac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}$ The voltage divider gives: $$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$ ### 3.2.2 Simplifying the Expression Multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$: $$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC}$$ Expanding the denominator: $$(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC = 1 + 2j\omega RC + (j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC = 1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 3j\omega RC$$ ### 3.2.3 Phase Condition For zero phase shift, the denominator's imaginary part must be zero, which occurs when: $$1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 0$$ Therefore: $$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega RC = 1$$ Solving for $f$: $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ ### 3.2.4 Magnitude Condition At $\omega RC = 1$, the feedback factor becomes: $$\beta = \frac{j}{j \cdot 3} = \frac{1}{3}$$ Therefore: $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = 3$$ For a non-inverting amplifier: $$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{R_f = 2 R_1}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Astable Multivibrator Frequency (using IC 741) ### 3.3.1 Circuit Operation The op-amp output saturates at $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$. The voltage at the non-inverting input is: $$V_+ = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{out} = \pm \beta V_{sat}$$ where $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ is the feedback fraction. The capacitor charges/discharges through $R$ toward $V_{out}$. ### 3.3.2 Charging Equation Assume output is at $+V_{sat}$. The capacitor voltage charges from $-\beta V_{sat}$ toward $+V_{sat}$: $$V_C(t) = V_{sat} - (V_{sat} + \beta V_{sat}) e^{-t/RC} = V_{sat}(1 - (1+\beta)e^{-t/RC})$$ ### 3.3.3 Switching Condition The op-amp switches when $V_C(t) = +\beta V_{sat}$. Setting: $$\beta V_{sat} = V_{sat}(1 - (1+\beta)e^{-T/2/RC})$$ Dividing by $V_{sat}$: $$\beta = 1 - (1+\beta)e^{-T/2/RC}$$ Solving: $$(1+\beta)e^{-T/2/RC} = 1 - \beta$$ $$e^{-T/2/RC} = \frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}$$ $$-\frac{T}{2RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}\right)$$ ### 3.3.4 Half Period $$\frac{T}{2} = RC \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$$ Substituting $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$: $$\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta} = \frac{1 + \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}}{1 - \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}} = \frac{2R_1+R_2}{R_2} = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_2}$$ ### 3.3.5 Full Period and Frequency $$T = 2RC \ln\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_2}\right)$$ $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2RC \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_2}\right)}}$$ ### 3.3.6 Special Case — When $R_1 = R_2 = R$ $$f = \frac{1}{2RC \ln 3} \approx \frac{1}{2.197 RC} \approx \frac{0.455}{RC}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Monostable Pulse Width (using IC 741) ### 3.4.1 Stable State In stable state, $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ and capacitor is uncharged (or fully discharged). The non-inverting input is at: $$V_+ = -\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} \cdot V_{sat} = -\beta V_{sat}$$ ### 3.4.2 After Triggering When triggered, $V_{out}$ jumps to $+V_{sat}$. The capacitor charges from 0 toward $+V_{sat}$. $$V_C(t) = V_{sat}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$$ ### 3.4.3 Switching Back The op-amp switches back when $V_C(t) = +\beta V_{sat}$: $$\beta V_{sat} = V_{sat}(1 - e^{-T/RC})$$ $$e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \beta$$ $$\boxed{T = RC \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-\beta}\right) = RC \ln\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)}$$ For $R_1 = R_2$: $$T = RC \ln 2 \approx 0.693 RC$$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example — Wien Bridge Oscillator Design > [!NOTE] > **Design Problem (Typical KTU Pattern):** Design a Wien bridge oscillator for $f = 2 \text{ kHz}$ using IC 741. **Step 1 — Choose Capacitor** For audio frequency range, select $C = 0.01 \mu F = 10 \text{ nF}$. **Step 2 — Calculate Resistor** From $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$: $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 2 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{4\pi \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$R = \frac{10^5}{4\pi} \approx 7957.7 \, \Omega$$ **Step 3 — Use Standard Value** Select $R = 7.87 \text{ k}\Omega$ (closest E12 value) or $R = 8.2 \text{ k}\Omega$. **Step 4 — Gain Resistors** For $A = 3$: $$R_f = 2 R_1$$ Choose $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$. **Step 5 — Verification** $$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 8200 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1940 \, \text{Hz} \approx 2 \, \text{kHz} \checkmark$$ --- ## 3.6 Worked Example — Astable Multivibrator Design > [!NOTE] > **Design Problem:** Design an astable multivibrator using IC 741 to generate a 1 kHz square wave. **Step 1 — Set Feedback Resistors** For symmetry, choose $R_1 = R_2 = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$. **Step 2 — Calculate RC** Using $f \approx 0.455/RC$: $$RC = \frac{0.455}{f} = \frac{0.455}{1000} = 4.55 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Choose Components** Select $C = 0.01 \mu F = 10 \text{ nF}$. Then: $$R = \frac{4.55 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-8}} = 45.5 \, \text{k}\Omega$$ Use $R = 47 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard value), then verify: $$f = \frac{1}{2 \times 47 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} \times \ln 3} = \frac{1}{2 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0986}$$ $$f = \frac{1}{1.033 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 968 \, \text{Hz} \approx 1 \, \text{kHz} \checkmark$$ # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — Circuit Schematics & Block Diagrams ## 4.1 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR AMP[Op-Amp IC 741
