[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Barkhausen criterion states two necessary conditions for sustained oscillations in a feedback system: 1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity. $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$ 2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be an integer multiple of $360°$ (or $0°$). $$\angle A\beta = 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$ If $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$, oscillations grow until clipping/distortion occurs. If $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$, oscillations decay and die out. **(1 mark each condition + 1 mark for explanation)** --- ### Question 2: Why does a Wien bridge oscillator require a gain of exactly 3? [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Wien network has a feedback factor of $\beta = 1/3$ at the resonant frequency. Applying the Barkhausen magnitude condition: $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \times \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = 3$$ If $A < 3$, oscillations will not start or will decay. If $A > 3$, the output will saturate and become distorted. Hence, the gain must be **exactly 3** for clean sine wave generation. **(2 marks for derivation + 1 mark for explanation)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 ### Question A: Design a Wien bridge oscillator using IC 741 to generate a sine wave of frequency 5 kHz. Derive the expression for frequency of oscillation and explain the role of feedback network. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] #### Part (a) — Derive the expression for frequency of oscillation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Wien Network Configuration:** The Wien bridge oscillator uses a series RC arm ($Z_1$) and parallel RC arm ($Z_2$): - $Z_1 = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ - $Z_2 = \dfrac{R \cdot (1/j\omega C)}{R + (1/j\omega C)} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$ **Step 2 — Feedback Factor:** The feedback factor is: $$\beta = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$ **Step 3 — Simplification:** Multiplying by $j\omega C$: $$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 4 — Expansion:** $$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + 2j\omega RC + (j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC} = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 3j\omega RC}$$ **Step 5 — Phase Condition:** For zero phase shift (oscillation condition), the real part of denominator must be zero: $$1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega^2 = \frac{1}{R^2 C^2}$$ **Step 6 — Frequency:** $$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ #### Part (b) — Design for 5 kHz and Role of Feedback (7 marks) **Step 1 — Choose Capacitor:** Select $C = 10 \text{ nF}$ (suitable for audio range). **Step 2 — Calculate Resistor:** $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{3.14 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3183 \, \Omega$$ **Step 3 — Use Standard Value:** Select $R = 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega$ (E12 series standard). **Step 4 — Verify Frequency:** $$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3300 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 4823 \, \text{Hz} \approx 4.8 \, \text{kHz}$$ **Step 5 — Gain Resistor Selection:** For non-inverting amplifier with $A = 3$: $$R_f = 2 R_1$$ Choose $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ → $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$. **Step 6 — Role of Feedback Network:** The Wien network provides **positive feedback** at the resonant frequency with $\beta = 1/3$ and zero phase shift. The non-inverting amplifier provides gain $A = 3$ to satisfy $|A\beta| = 1$. **(2 marks for design + 5 marks for role of feedback and complete design)** --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 ### Question B: Design an astable multivibrator using IC 741 to generate a 1 kHz square wave. Derive the expression for frequency of oscillation and explain the circuit operation with suitable waveforms. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] #### Part (a) — Derive the expression for frequency of oscillation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Circuit Configuration:** The op-amp has: - Positive feedback through voltage divider $R_1$ and $R_2$ (to non-inverting input) - Timing RC network at inverting input **Step 2 — Threshold Voltage:** $$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}, \quad V_+ = \pm \beta V_{sat}$$ **Step 3 — Charging Equation:** When output is at $+V_{sat}$, capacitor charges from $-\beta V_{sat}$ toward $+V_{sat}$: $$V_C(t) = V_{sat} - (V_{sat} + \beta V_{sat})e^{-t/RC}$$ **Step 4 — Switching Condition:** Switching occurs when $V_C(t) = +\beta V_{sat}$: $$\beta V_{sat} = V_{sat} - (1+\beta)V_{sat} \cdot e^{-T/2/RC}$$ **Step 5 — Solving:** $$(1+\beta)e^{-T/2/RC} = 1 - \beta$$ $$e^{-T/2/RC} = \frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}$$ **Step 6 — Half Period:** $$\frac{T}{2} = RC \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Full Period:** Substituting $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$: $$T = 2RC \ln\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_2}\right)$$ $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2RC \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_2}\right)}}$$ #### Part (b) — Design for 1 kHz and Operation Explanation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Choose Feedback Resistors:** For symmetric design, select $R_1 = R_2 = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$. **Step 2 — Calculate RC:** Using $f \approx 0.455/RC$: $$RC = \frac{0.455}{1000} = 4.55 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Component Selection:** Select $C = 0.01 \mu F$, then: $$R = \frac{4.55 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-8}} = 45.5 \, \text{k}\Omega$$ Use $R = 47 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard value). **Step 4 — Circuit Operation:** - **Initial State:** Assume $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$. The voltage at non-inverting input is $+\beta V_{sat}$. - **Charging:** Capacitor charges through $R$ toward $+V_{sat}$. - **Switching:** When $V_C$ reaches $+\beta V_{sat}$, the op-amp switches. Output becomes $-V_{sat}$. - **Reverse Charging:** Capacitor now discharges and charges in opposite direction. - **Switching Back:** When $V_C$ reaches $-\beta V_{sat}$, output switches back to $+V_{sat}$. - **Cycle Repeats:** Continuous square wave output. **Step 5 — Waveforms:** The output is a **symmetric square wave** between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$ with period $T = 1/f = 1 \text{ ms}$ for 1 kHz output. **(2 marks for derivation + 5 marks for design and operation)** --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Wrong gain for RC phase shift oscillator:** Students often write $A = 3$ (Wien) instead of $A = 29$ (RC phase shift). Each circuit has different gain requirements. > > 2. **Confusing frequencies:** RC phase shift uses $f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ (note the $\sqrt{6}$), while Wien bridge uses $f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. Forgetting $\sqrt{6}$ costs full marks. > > 3. **Sign errors in derivation:** The feedback factor $\beta$ is positive ($1/3$ for Wien) but negative ($-1/29$) for RC phase shift. The inverting amplifier's negative gain is crucial. > > 4. **Forgetting logarithm:** Astable frequency formula has $\ln(1 + 2R_1/R_2)$, not just $RC$. Students often write $f = 1/RC$ which is dimensionally and numerically wrong. > > 5. **Not verifying Barkhausen criterion:** Every oscillator design must explicitly state how both magnitude and phase conditions are satisfied. > > 6. **Incorrect saturation voltage:** Use $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13 \text{ V}$ for IC 741 with $\pm 15 \text{ V}$ supply (not $\pm 15 \text{ V}$ itself). --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Essential Points to Remember for KTU Exam:** ### Core Concepts - ✅ Barkhausen criterion: $|A\beta| = 1$ and $\angle A\beta = 360°n$ - ✅ IC 741 op-amp: 8-pin DIP, ±15 V supply, $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13 \text{ V}$ - ✅ All five circuits use IC 741 as the active element ### Frequency Formulas - ✅ RC Phase Shift: $f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$, Gain = 29 - ✅ Wien Bridge: $f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, Gain = 3 - ✅ Triangular: $f = \frac{R_2}{4R_1 RC}$ - ✅ Sawtooth: $f = \frac{1}{2RC}$ (symmetric) - ✅ Astable: $f = \frac{1}{2RC\ln(1 + 2R_1/R_2)}$ - ✅ Monostable: $T = RC\ln(1 + R_1/R_2)$ ### Circuit Distinctions - ✅ **Phase Shift**: Uses inverting amplifier, three RC sections - ✅ **Wien Bridge**: Uses non-inverting amplifier, two RC sections (series + parallel) - ✅ **Triangular**: Two op-amps (Schmitt + Integrator) - ✅ **Sawtooth**: Constant current charging + fast discharge - ✅ **Astable**: No stable state, continuous oscillation - ✅ **Monostable**: One stable state, triggered by external pulse ### Design Procedure - ✅ Choose capacitor first (standard value) - ✅ Calculate resistor from frequency formula - ✅ Select feedback/gain resistors based on $A$ requirement - ✅ Verify frequency after component selection - ✅ Check both Barkhausen conditions ### Lab Implementation Tips - ✅ Use dual power supply (±15 V) - ✅ Connect pins 1, 5 (offset null) to $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ potentiometer for offset adjustment - ✅ Use CRO with proper ground connection - ✅ Verify waveforms at each stage before final observation - ✅ Calculate practical vs theoretical frequency (allow 5–10% tolerance) ### Common Exam Questions - ✅ Derive frequency formula for any oscillator - ✅ Design for given frequency (find $R$ and $C$) - ✅ Explain circuit operation with waveforms - ✅ Compare different oscillator types - ✅ Calculate gain requirements for Barkhausen criterion

topics.title: Astable/Monostable multivibrator using IC 555 timer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL407 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable/Monostable multivibrator using IC 555 timer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The IC 555 Timer — Formal Definition

The **IC 555** is a highly stable integrated circuit used for generating accurate time delays or oscillations. It is an 8-pin device containing two comparators, a flip-flop (SR latch), a discharge transistor, and a resistive voltage divider network. It can operate in three primary modes: **Astable** (free-running oscillator), **Monostable** (one-shot), and **Bistable** (flip-flop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the Analog & Digital Electronics Lab, the 555 timer is universally tested through the **Astable Multivibrator** and **Monostable Multivibrator** experiments. You must memorize pin functions, design equations, and the working waveform shapes — these appear frequently in viva voce and record evaluations.

### Pin Configuration of IC 555

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | GND | Ground reference (**0 V**) |
| 2 | TRIGGER | Active-low input to lower comparator (threshold **1/3 Vcc**) |
| 3 | OUTPUT | Drives the load (up to **200 mA**) |
| 4 | RESET | Active-low master reset |
| 5 | CONTROL VOLTAGE | External modulation of threshold |
| 6 | THRESHOLD | Active-high input to upper comparator (**2/3 Vcc**) |
| 7 | DISCHARGE | Open-collector discharge path |
| 8 | Vcc | Supply voltage (**+5 V to +15 V**) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### Analogy 1: The Automatic Staircase Light
Imagine a staircase light that turns on the moment you step on the first stair and automatically turns off after you climb up. You trigger it once (Monostable), and it stays "on" for a fixed, predetermined duration before snapping back. The 555 in monostable mode behaves exactly like this — one trigger pulse, one timed output pulse.

### Analogy 2: The Waving Traffic Police
A traffic policeman blows a whistle, then signals "stop" for a duration, then "go" for another duration, repeating forever without anyone telling him to. This is an **Astable** multivibrator — it has no stable state, oscillating between HIGH and LOW continuously on its own, like a heart beating without external command.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Distinction to Memorize:**
> - **Astable** = No stable state → continuously oscillates (used as clock generators, LED flashers, tone generators).
> - **Monostable** = One stable state → produces a single pulse on trigger (used in timers, missing pulse detectors, debouncing circuits).
> - **Bistable** = Two stable states → acts as a flip-flop (not in our lab syllabus).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Block Diagram of IC 555

The 555 timer consists of:
1. A **resistive voltage divider** of three **5 kΩ** resistors (hence the name "555") that produces reference voltages of **1/3 Vcc** and **2/3 Vcc**.
2. **Comparator 1** (Lower): compares TRIGGER pin voltage with **1/3 Vcc**.
3. **Comparator 2** (Upper): compares THRESHOLD pin voltage with **2/3 Vcc**.
4. An **SR Flip-Flop** that stores the state.
5. A **discharge transistor** (open-collector NPN) connected to pin 7.
6. An **output stage** capable of sourcing/sinking **200 mA**.

## 2.2 Astable Multivibrator — Operating Logic

In the astable configuration, the 555 has **no external trigger input**. Pin 2 and pin 6 are tied together, and the capacitor **C** charges through **R1** and **R2**, then discharges through **R2** alone.

### Step-by-Step Working:

- **Initial State:** Capacitor **C** is uncharged. Voltage across C < **1/3 Vcc**, so the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop → OUTPUT goes HIGH. Discharge transistor Q1 is OFF.

- **Charging Phase:** C charges towards Vcc through the series combination **R1 + R2**. Charging continues until Vc reaches **2/3 Vcc** (upper threshold). When Vc = **2/3 Vcc**, the upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop → OUTPUT goes LOW. Discharge transistor Q1 turns ON.

- **Discharging Phase:** C now discharges through **R2** and the discharge transistor to ground. Discharging continues until Vc falls to **1/3 Vcc** (lower threshold). When Vc = **1/3 Vcc**, the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop → OUTPUT goes HIGH again. The cycle repeats forever.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it oscillates:** The capacitor voltage perpetually swings between **1/3 Vcc** and **2/3 Vcc**, never settling — hence the name "A-stable" (no stable state). This is the heart of every square-wave generator built with a 555.

## 2.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Operating Logic

In the monostable configuration, the 555 has **one stable state** (OUTPUT = LOW). A negative trigger pulse at pin 2 forces the output HIGH for a predetermined duration.

### Step-by-Step Working:

- **Stable State:** No trigger applied. Vc across C is **0 V** (capacitor discharged through pin 7). OUTPUT = LOW. Discharge transistor ON.

- **Trigger Arrives:** A negative pulse < **1/3 Vcc** at pin 2 SETS the flip-flop → OUTPUT goes HIGH. Discharge transistor turns OFF.

- **Timing Phase:** C now charges through external **R** towards Vcc. Charging continues until Vc = **2/3 Vcc**, at which point the upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop → OUTPUT returns LOW. The cycle ends, and the circuit returns to its stable state, waiting for the next trigger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from Astable:** Monostable requires an external trigger. It produces exactly one output pulse per trigger. The pulse width is determined by **R** and **C** only — not by R1 and R2 as in astable.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table below, the absolute value notation uses `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` symbol to prevent breaking the KTU markdown table parser.

| Parameter | Astable Formula | Monostable Formula | Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Charging Time (t_HIGH) | $0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C$ | Not applicable | seconds |
| Discharging Time (t_LOW) | $0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C$ | Not applicable | seconds |
| Total Time Period (T) | $0.693 \cdot (R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C$ | $1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ | seconds |
| Frequency (f) | $\dfrac{1.45}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C}$ | $\dfrac{0.91}{R \cdot C}$ | Hertz |
| Duty Cycle (D) | $\dfrac{(R_1 + R_2)}{(R_1 + 2R_2)} \times 100$ | Not applicable | percent |
| Output HIGH Level | $\approx V_{cc}$ | $\approx V_{cc}$ | volts |
| Output LOW Level | $\approx 0.0$ | $\approx 0.0$ | volts |
| Upper Threshold | $\dfrac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ | $\dfrac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ | volts |
| Lower Threshold | $\dfrac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ | volts |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Astable Applications:** Square-wave clock generators for digital logic, PWM controllers for motor speed control, LED flashers, tone generators in toys, pulse generators for laboratory instruments.
- **Monostable Applications:** Debouncing mechanical switch signals, missing pulse detectors in safety systems, time-delay relays, frequency dividers, sequential timing circuits in industrial automation.

> [!NOTE]
> The 555 timer is one of the **best-selling ICs in history** (billions sold). Its simplicity, robustness, and wide supply range (**+5 V to +15 V**) make it indispensable even in modern embedded designs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Astable Time Period

### Charging Phase Derivation (t_HIGH):

The capacitor charges from **V_lower = 1/3 Vcc** to **V_upper = 2/3 Vcc** through the series resistors **R1** and **R2**. The supply voltage is **Vcc**.

The general charging equation of a capacitor through a resistor is:

$$V_c(t) = V_{final} - (V_{final} - V_{initial}) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

Substituting the values for our circuit:

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc} - (V_{cc} - \tfrac{1}{3} V_{cc}) \cdot e^{-t / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]}$$

Simplifying the initial voltage difference:

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc} - \tfrac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]}$$

At time **t = t_HIGH**, the capacitor voltage reaches **2/3 Vcc**. So we set Vc(t_HIGH) = 2/3 Vcc:

$$\tfrac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \tfrac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t_{HIGH} / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]}$$

Rearranging to isolate the exponential term:

$$\tfrac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t_{HIGH} / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]} = V_{cc} - \tfrac{2}{3} V_{cc} = \tfrac{1}{3} V_{cc}$$

$$e^{-t_{HIGH} / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]} = \tfrac{(1/3) V_{cc}}{(2/3) V_{cc}} = \tfrac{1}{2}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-\dfrac{t_{HIGH}}{(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2) = -0.693$$

Therefore:

$$t_{HIGH} = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C$$

### Discharging Phase Derivation (t_LOW):

The capacitor discharges from **2/3 Vcc** to **1/3 Vcc** through resistor **R2** only (the discharge transistor acts as a short to ground).

The general discharging equation is:

$$V_c(t) = V_{initial} \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

Substituting:

$$V_c(t) = \tfrac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t / (R_2 \cdot C)}$$

At **t = t_LOW**, the voltage falls to **1/3 Vcc**:

$$\tfrac{1}{3} V_{cc} = \tfrac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t_{LOW} / (R_2 \cdot C)}$$

Isolating the exponential:

$$e^{-t_{LOW} / (R_2 \cdot C)} = \dfrac{(1/3) V_{cc}}{(2/3) V_{cc}} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

$$-\dfrac{t_{LOW}}{R_2 \cdot C} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -0.693$$

Therefore:

$$t_{LOW} = 0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C$$

### Total Time Period & Frequency:

$$T = t_{HIGH} + t_{LOW} = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C + 0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C}$$

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{0.693 \cdot (R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C} = \dfrac{1.45}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Monostable Pulse Width

The capacitor starts uncharged (Vc = 0) and charges through **R** towards **Vcc** until Vc reaches **2/3 Vcc**.

Using the charging equation with **V_initial = 0**:

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/RC})$$

At **t = T** (pulse width), Vc = **2/3 Vcc**:

$$\tfrac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} \cdot (1 - e^{-T/(RC)})$$

$$\tfrac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/(RC)}$$

$$e^{-T/(RC)} = 1 - \tfrac{2}{3} = \tfrac{1}{3}$$

$$-\dfrac{T}{RC} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right) = -1.0986 \approx -\ln(3)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{T = 1.0986 \cdot R \cdot C \approx 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical value **1.1** in the monostable formula comes from the natural logarithm of 3 (ln 3 ≈ 1.0986), while **0.693** in the astable formula comes from ln 2. This is a deep conceptual point examiners love to test.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Astable)

**Problem:** Design an astable multivibrator using IC 555 with **R1 = 4.7 kΩ**, **R2 = 6.8 kΩ**, **C = 0.1 μF**, and **Vcc = +12 V**. Calculate t_HIGH, t_LOW, T, f, and duty cycle.

**Solution:**

Computing t_HIGH:

$$t_{HIGH} = 0.693 \times (4.7 \times 10^3 + 6.8 \times 10^3) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_{HIGH} = 0.693 \times 11.5 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_{HIGH} = 0.693 \times 1.15 \times 10^{-3} = 7.97 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 797 \, \mu s$$

Computing t_LOW:

$$t_{LOW} = 0.693 \times 6.8 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_{LOW} = 0.693 \times 6.8 \times 10^{-4} = 4.71 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 471 \, \mu s$$

Computing T:

$$T = t_{HIGH} + t_{LOW} = 797 + 471 = 1268 \, \mu s \approx 1.27 \text{ ms}$$

Computing f:

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{1.27 \times 10^{-3}} = 787.4 \text{ Hz} \approx 787 \text{ Hz}$$

Computing Duty Cycle:

$$D = \dfrac{t_{HIGH}}{T} \times 100 = \dfrac{797}{1268} \times 100 = 62.85\%$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Monostable)

**Problem:** Design a monostable multivibrator with pulse width **T = 5 ms** using **C = 1 μF**. Find R.

**Solution:**

Using the formula T = 1.1 RC:

$$5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.1 \times R \times 1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$R = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^3}{1.1} = 4545.45 \, \Omega \approx 4.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Design Trade-offs

| Design Goal | Astable Adjustment | Effect |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Increase frequency | Decrease R1, R2, or C | f increases proportionally |
| Achieve 50% duty cycle | Make R1 << R2 (use diode across R2) | Bypasses R1 during charge |
| Increase pulse width (Monostable) | Increase R or C | T increases linearly |
| Improve frequency stability | Use stable components, low-leakage C | Tighter output waveform |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IC 555 Internal Block Diagram (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["Vcc Supply +5V to +15V"]:::supply
    node2["Resistive Divider 3 x 5k ohm"]:::divider
    node3["Lower Comparator Ref 1/3 Vcc"]:::comp
    node4["Upper Comparator Ref 2/3 Vcc"]:::comp
    node5["SR Flip Flop Latch"]:::latch
    node6["Output Driver Buffer 200mA"]:::out
    node7["Discharge Transistor NPN Open Collector"]:::trans
    node8["Pin 3 Output"]:::pin
    node9["Pin 7 Discharge"]:::pin
    node10["Pin 2 Trigger"]:::pin
    node11["Pin 6 Threshold"]:::pin

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node10 --> node3
    node11 --> node4
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node9

    classDef supply fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef divider fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef latch fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pin fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Astable Multivibrator — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Vcc +12V"]:::pwr --> B["R1 Resistor"]:::r
    B --> C["R2 Resistor"]:::r
    C --> D["Junction of Pin 6 and Pin 7"]:::node
    D --> E["Pin 2 Trigger"]:::pin
    D --> F["Pin 6 Threshold"]:::pin
    D --> G["Pin 7 Discharge Path"]:::pin
    D --> H["Capacitor C to GND"]:::cap
    I["IC 555 Timer Core"]:::ic --> J["Pin 3 Output"]:::out
    J --> K["Oscilloscope Channel 1"]:::scope
    H --> L["Oscilloscope Channel 2"]:::scope

    classDef pwr fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef r fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#FFFF00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pin fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ic fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef out fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef scope fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Vcc +12V"]:::pwr --> B["R Timing Resistor"]:::r
    B --> C["Pin 7 Discharge"]:::pin
    C --> D["Pin 6 Threshold"]:::pin
    D --> E["Capacitor C to GND"]:::cap
    F["External Trigger Pulse Generator"]:::trig --> G["Pin 2 Trigger Input"]:::pin
    H["IC 555 Timer Core"]:::ic --> I["Pin 3 Output Single Pulse"]:::out
    I --> J["Oscilloscope Display"]:::scope

    classDef pwr fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef r fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pin fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trig fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ic fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef out fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef scope fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Waveform Timing Diagram (Astable) — Text-Based Schematic

```
   Vc (Capacitor Voltage)
    |
2/3 Vcc -----+        +---+        +---+        +---
             |        |   |        |   |        |
             |        |   |        |   |        |
1/3 Vcc -----+----+   |   |   +----+   |   +----+
             |    |   |   |   |        |   |
    0V ------+----+---+---+---+--------+---+-----> time
                t_LOW    t_HIGH   t_LOW  t_HIGH

   Output (Pin 3)
    |
 Vcc ----+        +---+        +---+        +---
         |        |   |        |   |        |
         |        |   |        |   |        |
   0V ---+----+   |   |   +----+   |   +----+
            t_LOW    t_HIGH   t_LOW  t_HIGH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Takeaway:** The capacitor voltage is a **sawtooth-like waveform** that exponentially charges and discharges between **1/3 Vcc** and **2/3 Vcc**, while the output is a **rectangular square wave** that toggles HIGH during charging and LOW during discharging.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — Define an Astable Multivibrator. List any two applications.

**Model Answer:**
An astable multivibrator is a free-running oscillator that has **no stable state**. It continuously switches between two quasi-stable states (HIGH and LOW) without any external trigger, producing a rectangular wave output. The output frequency and duty cycle are determined by external RC components.

**Applications:**
1. Square-wave clock generator for digital circuits.
2. LED flasher / tone generator / pulse generator for laboratory use.

*(3 marks: 2 for definition, 1 for applications)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Differentiate between Monostable and Astable Multivibrators.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Monostable | Astable |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Number of stable states | One (OUTPUT = LOW) | Zero |
| External trigger required | Yes | No |
| Output per trigger | One pulse | Continuous oscillation |
| Timing components | One R and one C | R1, R2, and C |
| Output frequency formula | T = 1.1 RC | f = 1.45 / (R1+2R2)C |
| Typical use | Timers, debouncers | Clock generators, flashers |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** The single most common mistake students make is writing the **same formula for both astable and monostable** modes. The astable formula contains **(R1 + 2R2)**, while the monostable formula is simply **1.1 RC**. Mixing these up costs 4–5 marks instantly. Another common error is forgetting to convert resistor values into **ohms** and capacitance into **farads** before substituting into the formula — this loses another 2 marks. Always show the unit conversion explicitly.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **Astable Multivibrator** using IC 555 timer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an astable multivibrator using IC 555 to generate a **frequency of 1 kHz** with a **duty cycle of 75%**. Assume **C = 0.1 μF**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

A 555 astable multivibrator is constructed by connecting pins 2 and 6 together, with a capacitor **C** from that junction to ground, and resistors **R1** and **R2** forming a charging path from Vcc to the junction. Pin 5 is bypassed to ground with a 0.01 μF capacitor, and pin 4 (RESET) is tied to Vcc to disable reset.

**Working:**
1. Initially, C is uncharged. Since Vc < 1/3 Vcc, the lower comparator SETS the internal flip-flop. Output goes HIGH and the discharge transistor (pin 7) is OFF.
2. C now charges through R1 and R2 towards Vcc.
3. When Vc reaches 2/3 Vcc, the upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop. Output goes LOW and the discharge transistor turns ON.
4. C now discharges through R2 (and pin 7) to ground.
5. When Vc falls to 1/3 Vcc, the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop again, and the cycle repeats indefinitely.

**Output Equations:**

$$t_{HIGH} = 0.693 (R_1 + R_2) C, \quad t_{LOW} = 0.693 R_2 C$$

$$T = 0.693 (R_1 + 2R_2) C, \quad f = \frac{1.45}{(R_1 + 2R_2) C}$$

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:** f = 1 kHz, D = 75%, C = 0.1 μF

**Step 1 — Calculate the time period T:**

$$T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{1000} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Express duty cycle in terms of t_HIGH and T:**

$$D = \dfrac{t_{HIGH}}{T} \times 100 = 75\%$$

Therefore:

$$t_{HIGH} = 0.75 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 0.75 \text{ ms} = 750 \, \mu s$$

$$t_{LOW} = T - t_{HIGH} = 1 \text{ ms} - 0.75 \text{ ms} = 0.25 \text{ ms} = 250 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Use the t_LOW formula to find R2:**

$$t_{LOW} = 0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C$$

$$0.25 \times 10^{-3} = 0.693 \times R_2 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$R_2 = \dfrac{0.25 \times 10^{-3}}{0.693 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{0.25 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0693 \times 10^{-6}} = 3607.5 \, \Omega \approx 3.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Use the t_HIGH formula to find R1:**

$$t_{HIGH} = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C$$

$$0.75 \times 10^{-3} = 0.693 \times (R_1 + 3607.5) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$R_1 + 3607.5 = \dfrac{0.75 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0693 \times 10^{-6}} = 10822.5 \, \Omega$$

$$R_1 = 10822.5 - 3607.5 = 7215 \, \Omega \approx 7.2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Verification:**

$$T = 0.693 \times (7215 + 2 \times 3607.5) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T = 0.693 \times 14430 \times 10^{-6} = 9.998 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 1 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** R1 = **7.2 kΩ**, R2 = **3.6 kΩ**, C = 0.1 μF.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Monostable Multivibrator** using IC 555 timer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A monostable 555 circuit uses **R = 100 kΩ** and **C = 10 μF**. Calculate the output pulse width. If the trigger pulse is a **10 μs** negative pulse, comment on its validity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

A 555 monostable multivibrator is constructed by connecting pin 6 (THRESHOLD) to the junction of an external resistor **R** and capacitor **C** to ground. Pin 2 (TRIGGER) receives an external negative trigger pulse. Pin 4 (RESET) is tied HIGH to Vcc. Pin 7 (DISCHARGE) is connected to the same R-C junction as pin 6.

**Working:**
1. **Stable State:** In the absence of a trigger, the output is LOW. The discharge transistor is ON, keeping C shorted to ground. So Vc = 0.
2. **Trigger Event:** A negative trigger pulse < 1/3 Vcc is applied to pin 2. This SETS the internal flip-flop → output goes HIGH, and the discharge transistor turns OFF.
3. **Timing Phase:** C now charges through R towards Vcc. The output remains HIGH throughout this charging.
4. **End of Pulse:** When Vc reaches 2/3 Vcc, the upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop → output returns LOW. The discharge transistor turns ON, rapidly discharging C to ground.
5. The circuit returns to its stable state, awaiting the next trigger.

**Output Pulse Width:**

$$T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$$

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Calculate the pulse width T:**

$$T = 1.1 \times R \times C = 1.1 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T = 1.1 \times 100000 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 1.1 \times 1 = 1.1 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Comment on trigger validity:**

The trigger pulse width is **10 μs**, and the output pulse width is **1.1 s**.

For the monostable to function correctly, the trigger pulse width must be **much shorter** than the output pulse width. Here, **10 μs << 1.1 s** (by a factor of **1,10,000**), so the trigger pulse is **perfectly valid**. The circuit will respond reliably to this short negative-going trigger and produce a clean 1.1-second output pulse.

**Additional Note:** If the trigger pulse were longer than the output pulse width, the output would be prematurely cut off, leading to incorrect operation. The general rule is: trigger pulse width < output pulse width / 10.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam!**

- The IC 555 contains **two comparators**, **one SR flip-flop**, **one discharge transistor**, and a **resistive divider of three 5 kΩ resistors**.
- Threshold voltages: **1/3 Vcc** (lower, for trigger) and **2/3 Vcc** (upper, for threshold).
- **Astable** → no stable state → no external trigger needed → f = **1.45 / [(R1 + 2R2) C]**.
- **Monostable** → one stable state → external trigger required → T = **1.1 RC**.
- The constant **0.693 ≈ ln 2** and the constant **1.1 ≈ ln 3** arise naturally from the 1/3 and 2/3 Vcc thresholds.
- In astable mode, the **capacitor charges through R1 + R2** but **discharges through R2 only** — this is why duty cycle can never be exactly 50% in a standard 555 astable circuit.
- Pin 5 (Control Voltage) can be used to **modulate the output frequency** by injecting an external voltage that shifts the 2/3 Vcc threshold.
- Pin 4 (RESET) is **active LOW** — tying it to Vcc disables reset, tying it to ground forces output LOW.
- Pin 7 (DISCHARGE) is an **open-collector** output — it can sink current but cannot source.
- Pin 3 (OUTPUT) can **source or sink up to 200 mA** — enough to drive small relays, LEDs, or buzzers directly.
- The 555 can operate from **+5 V to +15 V** supply, making it compatible with both TTL and CMOS logic families.
- For KTU lab record purposes, always draw the **circuit diagram**, the **capacitor voltage waveform**, and the **output waveform** on the same time axis to demonstrate the timing relationship.
- Common practical design values: R1, R2 in the range **1 kΩ to 1 MΩ**, C in the range **1 nF to 1000 μF**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of PLL using IC 565
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL407 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of PLL using IC 565

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of PLL (Phase Locked Loop)

A **Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a closed-loop negative feedback control system in which the phase of an internally generated oscillator output signal is forced to track (or "lock" onto) the phase of an externally applied reference/input signal. Once the loop is locked, the output frequency becomes an exact multiple (or rational fraction) of the input reference frequency, and the static phase error is held constant.

When implemented using the **monolithic IC 565** (Signetics NE/SE 565), the PLL becomes a self-contained, low-cost, general-purpose building block operating typically from **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz** with a single supply up to **±12 V** (or +12 V single-ended).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCMRL407 — Module Experiment 2):**
> The student is expected to *design*, *wire up*, and *verify experimentally* the operation of a PLL using IC 565. The "design" refers to computing the external component values (R₁, C₁, R₂, C₂) from the required free-running frequency, lock range, and capture range.

## 1.2 The Three Building Blocks of a PLL (KTU Mandatory)

Every PLL — including the one inside IC 565 — contains exactly three fundamental sub-systems:

| Block | Full Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **PD** | Phase Detector | Compares the input signal f_in with the VCO output f_out and produces an error voltage proportional to their phase difference. |
| **LPF** | Low-Pass Filter | Removes the high-frequency sum components in the PD output, leaving a smooth DC control voltage. |
| **VCO** | Voltage-Controlled Oscillator | Generates an output whose frequency f_out depends linearly on the applied control voltage V_c from the LPF. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A PLL is a **negative feedback system in the frequency/phase domain**, not in the voltage domain. The "feedback signal" is not a voltage but a frequency (or phase).

## 1.3 The IC 565 — A Self-Contained PLL Chip

The **IC 565** is a 14-pin DIP monolithic PLL that contains, on a single silicon die:
- A high-gain balanced **Phase Detector (PD)** of the double-balanced mixer type,
- A highly stable **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** with very linear frequency-vs-voltage characteristics,
- An internal **low-pass filter amplifier** (which only needs an external capacitor C₂ and resistor R₂ to be completed),
- Two 3.6 kΩ internal biasing resistors (one sets the VCO timing, the other the DC bias for the PD).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key IC 565 Specifications (must remember for KTU viva):**
> - Operating frequency range: **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz**
> - VCO linearity: typically **0.2 %** of full scale
> - VCO free-running temperature coefficient: **300 ppm/°C** (excellent stability)
> - Operating supply: **±6 V to ±12 V** dual or **+10 V to +24 V** single
> - TTL/DTL compatible outputs

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Octopus Tuning Its Drum"

Imagine a **musician (VCO)** playing a drum at a steady rhythm (the VCO frequency). A **distant second musician (input signal)** plays another drum, but the first musician cannot hear the second directly — he can only feel a **vibration sensor (Phase Detector)** on his own drum. The sensor compares the timing of vibrations from his own drum with vibrations transmitted through the floor from the distant drum, producing a **correction signal (LPF output)** telling him to speed up or slow down.

If the distant drum is within "**hearing range**" (the **Lock Range**), the first musician unconsciously adjusts his speed until both drums beat in perfect synchronism — this is the **locked** state. If the distant drum speeds up or slows down *within* a narrower band (the **Capture Range**), the first musician can *re-synchronize*; outside it, he loses track.

The IC 565 is exactly this "musical pairing" of input and VCO signals, all in one chip.

> [!TIP]
> **Mental hook:** *Lock range = "How far can the input wander before losing lock?"*  
> *Capture range = "How far can a wandering input be and still be grabbed back into lock?"*  
> Always remember: **Capture Range < Lock Range**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of a PLL built around IC 565 can be broken into five clearly distinguishable phases:

1. **Input is applied (f_in) to the Phase Detector** at Pin 11. The VCO output (f_out) is internally fed back to the second input of the PD at Pin 12.

2. **Phase Detector produces an error voltage V_d** whose average value is proportional to the *phase difference* φ between f_in and f_out:
$$V_d = K_d \cdot (\phi_{in} - \phi_{out})$$

3. **Low-Pass Filter removes the high-frequency sum component** (2f components) from V_d, producing a slowly varying control voltage V_c. The LPF on IC 565 is realized by the external R₂–C₂ connected between Pins 6 and 7 (with an internal 3.6 kΩ resistor already present at Pin 7).

4. **VCO shifts its output frequency f_out** in proportion to V_c:
$$f_{out} = f_0 + K_0 \cdot V_c$$

where f_0 is the *free-running frequency* of the VCO and K_0 is the VCO gain (Hz/V).

5. **Negative feedback pulls f_out toward f_in.** The system reaches equilibrium (lock) when f_out = f_in and the phase error is constant. At lock, the PD output is at its *center voltage* and the VCO runs exactly at the input frequency.

## 2.2 The Three Frequency Specifications (Critical for Design)

For any PLL design, three frequency quantities must be computed, measured, and clearly distinguished:

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free-running frequency** | f_0 | VCO frequency when no input is applied (V_c = 0) | Set by R₁, C₁ |
| **Lock Range** | ±Δf_L | Total band of input frequencies over which the loop, **once locked**, remains locked (also called *tracking range* or *hold-in range*) | Set by V_cc and f_0 |
| **Capture Range** | ±Δf_C | Band of input frequencies over which the loop, **initially unlocked**, can acquire lock (also called *acquisition range*) | Set by LPF (R₂ C₂) and Δf_L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Captured vs. Locked distinction (favourite viva question!):**
> - A loop is *captured* when it first achieves lock starting from an unlocked condition.
> - A loop is *locked* when it is already in lock and the input is slowly varied.
> - Therefore: **Δf_C ≤ Δf_L** always.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (IC 565 Specific)

The following four formulas constitute the **entire** mathematical design space for the IC 565 PLL. Memorise these with their units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | VCO free-running frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1.4}{4 \, R_1 C_1}$ | f_0 in Hz; R_1 in Ω; C_1 in F. R_1 is the external resistor between V_cc and Pin 8. C_1 is between Pin 9 and ground. |
| 2 | Lock range (one-sided) | $\Delta f_L = \dfrac{8 \, f_0}{V_{CC}}$ | Δf_L in Hz; V_cc in volts. Total lock range is 2 Δf_L. |
| 3 | Capture range (one-sided) | $\Delta f_C = \pm \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi \, \Delta f_L}{R_2 \, C_2}}$ | Δf_C in Hz; R_2 in Ω (internal 3.6 kΩ in series); C_2 in F. |
| 4 | VCO sensitivity | $K_0 = \dfrac{8 \, f_0}{V_{CC}}$ (in Hz/V) | Same numerical value as Δf_L numerically; it represents the slope df/dV. |
| 5 | PD sensitivity (typical) | $K_d \approx \dfrac{1.4}{\pi}$ V/rad | Built into the chip. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall:** The capture-range formula uses the **internal 3.6 kΩ** resistor of the IC — not the external R₂ that students sometimes mistakenly include as a separate value. In the standard external connection, R₂ is the external resistor at Pin 7 only when a current-limiting resistor is needed; the formula R_eq = 3.6 kΩ (internal) is the one to use in the LPF.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **FM demodulation:** The instantaneous frequency deviation of an FM signal is converted by the PLL into a proportional voltage variation at the LPF output — the basis of every FM radio IF stage.
- **Frequency synthesis:** Cascading PLLs with digital dividers in the feedback path generates precise multiples of a crystal reference (used in mobile phone base stations, signal generators).
- **FSK demodulation:** A PLL tuned to the mark frequency stays locked only during marks and loses lock during spaces — the PD output becomes a clean digital bit stream.
- **Motor speed control & AM demodulation (carrier recovery):** The PLL re-generates the suppressed carrier in a synchronous AM detector.
- **Tone decoding (NE/SE 567):** A close cousin of the 565, the 567 has a built-in quadrature phase detector for tone-decoder applications.

## 2.5 Why IC 565 Specifically?

Before the 565, PLLs were built from four or five op-amps, multipliers and a separate VCO — bulky, expensive and unstable. The 565 integrates all of this into a 14-pin IC, providing:
- An order-of-magnitude better temperature stability than discrete designs,
- Simplified external design (only four components: R₁, C₁, R₂, C₂),
- Predictable, published formulas — making it ideal for laboratory design verification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the VCO Free-Running Frequency f_0

The internal VCO of IC 565 is essentially a **Schmitt-trigger astable multivibrator** whose threshold voltages are derived from the supply V_cc. The capacitor C_1 charges towards V_cc through the timing resistor R_1 (external) + R_int (internal ≈ 3.6 kΩ), and discharges symmetrically.

The charging time from 0.25 V_cc to 0.75 V_cc through an effective resistance R_eff = R_1 + R_int is:

$$t_{charge} = -R_{eff} \, C_1 \ln\left(\frac{V_{cc} - 0.75 V_{cc}}{V_{cc} - 0.25 V_{cc}}\right)$$

$$t_{charge} = -R_{eff} \, C_1 \ln\left(\frac{0.25}{0.75}\right) = R_{eff} C_1 \ln 3$$

The total period is twice this (charge + discharge are equal by symmetry), so:

$$T_0 = 2 \, R_{eff} \, C_1 \ln 3$$

Substituting $\ln 3 \approx 1.0986$ and the internal resistance contribution already absorbed in the constant:

$$T_0 \approx \frac{4 R_1 C_1}{1.4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1.4}{4 R_1 C_1}$$

This is the published IC 565 result. **Note:** The factor 1.4 already folds in the internal 3.6 kΩ; hence in design we use R_1 as the *purely external* resistor.

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1.4}{4 \, R_1 \, C_1} \;\; \text{Hz}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Lock Range Δf_L

Inside the IC 565, the VCO is steered by a differential pair whose transconductance is set by V_cc. The maximum frequency swing around f_0 corresponds to the VCO control voltage saturating at ±V_cc/2 (because the internal PD cannot produce more than ±V_cc/2 at its output). Therefore:

$$f_{max} = f_0 + \frac{8 f_0}{V_{cc}} \cdot \frac{V_{cc}}{2} = f_0 + 4 f_0 = 5 f_0$$

$$f_{min} = f_0 - 4 f_0 = -3 f_0 \;\; (\text{not physical, but } f_0 - 4f_0 = 0 \text{ in practice})$$

The one-sided lock range is therefore:

$$\Delta f_L = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{cc}}$$

And the *total* lock range (both sides of f_0) is $2\Delta f_L$:

$$\boxed{\Delta f_L = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{cc}} \;\; \text{Hz (one-sided)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design implication:** To *increase* the lock range, you can either (a) raise f_0, or (b) lower V_cc. Conversely, locking the loop more tightly requires higher V_cc.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Capture Range Δf_C

The capture range depends on the LPF corner frequency f_LPF:

$$f_{LPF} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$$

The IC 565 capture range formula (published in the Signetics datasheet) is:

$$\Delta f_C = \pm \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{2\pi \, \Delta f_L}{R_2 C_2}}$$

Algebraic manipulation yields the alternate form often given in textbooks:

$$\Delta f_C = \pm \sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L}{2\pi R_2 C_2}}$$

Both are mathematically equivalent. The form to use is dictated by which quantities you know. In IC 565 the **internal 3.6 kΩ** acts as R_2:

$$R_2 = 3.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

## 3.4 Complete Worked Design Example

> **Design Problem (typical KTU style):**
> Design a PLL using IC 565 to operate from a +12 V single supply with the following specifications:
> - Free-running frequency: **f_0 = 1 kHz**
> - Lock range: **±1 kHz** (total = 2 kHz)
> - Capture range: **±200 Hz** (total = 400 Hz)

### Step 1 — Verify the supply voltage
For Δf_L = 1 kHz at f_0 = 1 kHz:
$$\Delta f_L = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{cc}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_{cc} = \frac{8 f_0}{\Delta f_L} = \frac{8 \times 1000}{1000} = 8 \text{ V}$$
The required V_cc = 8 V. Since the design asks for +12 V, the *resulting* lock range will be lower. We can either (a) accept the resulting Δf_L, or (b) use a regulated 8 V. The standard KTU design is to compute the *achieved* Δf_L for the given V_cc and continue. We will use V_cc = 8 V to honour the spec — set a zener or a regulator.

### Step 2 — Choose C_1 (a value within the practical range 0.001 µF to 10 µF)
Pick a standard value:
$$C_1 = 0.01 \;\; \mu F = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate R_1 from the f_0 formula
$$R_1 = \frac{1.4}{4 \, f_0 \, C_1} = \frac{1.4}{4 \times 1000 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$R_1 = \frac{1.4}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.4}{0.00004} = 35000 \;\; \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_1 = 35 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

A 35 kΩ resistor is well within the recommended range of 2 kΩ to 100 kΩ for IC 565.

### Step 4 — Compute achieved lock range
$$\Delta f_L = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{cc}} = \frac{8 \times 1000}{8} = 1000 \text{ Hz} \;\; \checkmark$$

### Step 5 — Choose C_2 from the capture-range equation
Using the published formula with R_2 = 3.6 kΩ (internal) and Δf_L = 1 kHz:

$$200 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi \times 1000}{3600 \times C_2}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$(200 \times 2\pi)^2 = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{3600 \times C_2}$$

$$(400\pi)^2 = \frac{2000\pi}{3600 \, C_2}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{2000\pi}{3600 \times (400\pi)^2} = \frac{2000}{3600 \times 400^2 \times \pi}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{2000}{3600 \times 160000 \times 3.1416} = \frac{2000}{1.81 \times 10^9}$$

$$C_2 \approx 1.10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} \approx 1.1 \;\; \mu F$$

Choosing the **nearest standard value**:

$$\boxed{C_2 = 1 \;\; \mu F}$$

This gives a slightly larger capture range than designed, which is acceptable in laboratory practice.

### Step 6 — Design Summary Table

| Component / Parameter | Designed Value | Standard Value Used |
|---|---|---|
| V_cc | 8 V (regulated) | +12 V (lab) → Δf_L = 667 Hz achieved |
| f_0 | 1 kHz | 1 kHz |
| C_1 | 0.01 µF | 0.01 µF |
| R_1 | 35 kΩ | 33 kΩ + 2 kΩ (pot) for trimming |
| C_2 | 1.1 µF | 1 µF (electrolytic, non-polar orientation) |
| R_2 | — | Internal 3.6 kΩ of IC 565 |
| Achieved Δf_L | 1 kHz | 667 Hz @ +12 V |
| Achieved Δf_C | 200 Hz | ≈ 220 Hz |

## 3.5 Procedure for Experimental Verification

1. **Wire the circuit** on a breadboard as per the pin diagram in Section 4. Mount IC 565 on a 14-pin IC base.
2. **Apply V_cc = ±6 V dual** (or +12 V single via a virtual ground formed by two 10 kΩ equal resistors across 12 V, with a 10 µF decoupling cap).
3. **Measure the free-running frequency** at Pin 4 using a frequency counter. No input signal applied.
4. **Apply a 1 V peak-to-peak sine signal from a function generator** at Pin 11. Set its frequency near f_0 = 1 kHz.
5. **Vary the input frequency** slowly from below f_0 to above f_0 in steps of 50 Hz. Note the *output* frequency at Pin 4 and *PD output* voltage at Pin 2. The PD output stays roughly constant when locked.
6. **Identify the lower and upper lock limits** — frequencies at which the output suddenly loses lock (the output frequency at Pin 4 stops following the input). The difference between these is 2Δf_L.
7. **Starting from an unlocked condition** (e.g., input at 700 Hz), slowly sweep the input upward. Note the frequency at which lock is first *acquired* — this is one edge of the capture range. Repeat from above. The difference is 2Δf_C. Verify 2Δf_C < 2Δf_L.
8. **Plot graphs** of (a) f_out vs f_in (showing the lock plateau), and (b) V_PD vs f_in (showing the triangular "S-curve" characteristic).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic PLL

```mermaid
graph LR
    inNode[Input Signal fin Pin 11]
    pdNode[Phase Detector]
    lpfNode[Low Pass Filter R2 C2]
    vcoNode[Voltage Controlled Oscillator VCO]
    outNode[Output Signal fout Pin 4]
    fbNode[Internal Feedback to Phase Detector]
    vccNode[Vcc Supply Pin 1]
    gndNode[Ground Pin 10]
    ctrlNode[Control Voltage Vc at Pin 7]

    inNode --> pdNode
    vcoNode --> fbNode
    fbNode --> pdNode
    pdNode --> lpfNode
    lpfNode --> ctrlNode
    ctrlNode --> vcoNode
    vcoNode --> outNode
    vccNode --> vcoNode
    gndNode --> vcoNode
    gndNode --> pdNode
    vccNode --> pdNode
```

## 4.2 IC 565 — Pin-Level Functional Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph TB
    pin1[Pin 1 Vcc Plus Supply]
    pin2[Pin 2 Phase Detector Output]
    pin3[Pin 3 VCO External Input]
    pin4[Pin 4 VCO Output Square Wave]
    pin5[Pin 5 VCO Control Voltage Node]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper Pattern]
**Define a Phase Locked Loop (PLL). List the three basic building blocks of a PLL.**

**Model Answer:**
A Phase Locked Loop (PLL) is a closed-loop electronic control system that synchronises the output frequency of a voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) with the frequency of an applied input reference signal by means of a phase-comparison feedback mechanism.

The three basic building blocks are:
1. **Phase Detector (PD)** — produces an error voltage proportional to the phase difference between the input signal and the VCO output.
2. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** — removes high-frequency components of the PD output to yield a smooth DC control voltage.
3. **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** — generates an output whose frequency is a linear function of the applied control voltage.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper Pattern]
**What is the difference between the Lock Range and the Capture Range of a PLL? Which is always smaller and why?**

**Model Answer:**
- The **Lock Range (Δf_L)** is the total band of input frequencies over which the PLL, *once locked*, remains in lock as the input frequency is slowly varied. It depends mainly on the maximum DC swing the PD can deliver to the VCO and on V_cc.
- The **Capture Range (Δf_C)** is the band of input frequencies over which the PLL, *starting from an unlocked condition*, can acquire lock. It depends on the bandwidth of the LPF as well as Δf_L.
- The **Capture Range is always smaller than the Lock Range (Δf_C < Δf_L)** because the LPF attenuates the beat-frequency component produced by the PD; only if the input frequency is close enough to f_0 that the beat frequency falls *within* the LPF pass-band can the loop acquire lock. Once locked, the loop can be pushed further because the PD then produces a steady (DC) error voltage, which is not filtered out.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Phase Locked Loop. Discuss briefly the four states of a PLL (free-running, capture, lock, lock-out). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a PLL using IC 565 to meet the following specifications: free-running frequency **f_0 = 5 kHz**, lock range **±2 kHz**, capture range **±400 Hz**. Assume V_cc = +10 V. Mention all component values (R₁, C₁, C₂). Verify that 2Δf_C < 2Δf_L. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Block diagram:** Draw the four blocks (PD → LPF → VCO) with input f_in feeding PD, VCO output f_out feeding back to PD. (Diagram marks)

**Operation:**
The input signal f_in is fed to one input of the Phase Detector, while the VCO output f_out is internally fed to the other input. The PD multiplies the two signals and produces a difference-frequency (beat) component. The LPF removes the high-frequency sum, leaving a slowly varying DC error voltage V_c. This V_c steers the VCO frequency toward f_in. When f_out = f_in and the phase error is constant, the loop is said to be in **lock**.

**Four states of the PLL:**
1. **Free-running:** No input applied; VCO oscillates at its natural f_0.
2. **Capture:** Input applied and within Δf_C of f_0; loop is acquiring lock; PD output is a low-frequency beat that gets filtered.
3. **Lock (tracking):** Input is within ±Δf_L of f_0 and loop is locked; PD output is a constant DC, VCO tracks input exactly.
4. **Lock-out (out-of-lock):** Input deviates beyond ±Δf_L; loop cannot follow; VCO returns to f_0.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Verify lock-range capability:**
Required Δf_L = 2 kHz, f_0 = 5 kHz, V_cc = 10 V.

$$\Delta f_L^{achieved} = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{cc}} = \frac{8 \times 5000}{10} = 4000 \text{ Hz} = 4 \text{ kHz}$$

Since 4 kHz > 2 kHz required, the supply is *adequate* (it actually delivers more than asked). *(Note: To reduce the achieved lock range to exactly 2 kHz, you would need V_cc = 20 V, which exceeds the IC rating — so we accept 4 kHz as the design outcome and explicitly state the caveat.)*

**Step 2 — Choose C₁:** Let C₁ = 0.001 µF = 10⁻⁹ F (standard value, good for 5 kHz).

**Step 3 — Calculate R₁:**
$$R_1 = \frac{1.4}{4 f_0 C_1} = \frac{1.4}{4 \times 5000 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.4}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 70000 \;\; \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_1 = 70 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

**Step 4 — Choose C₂ to get Δf_C = 400 Hz:** Use the internal 3.6 kΩ as R₂.

$$400 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi \times 4000}{3600 \times C_2}}$$

$$(400 \times 2\pi)^2 = \frac{2\pi \times 4000}{3600 \, C_2}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{2\pi \times 4000}{3600 \times (800\pi)^2} = \frac{4000}{3600 \times 400^2 \times \pi} = \frac{4000}{3600 \times 160000 \times 3.1416}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{4000}{1.81 \times 10^9} \approx 2.21 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$$

$$\boxed{C_2 \approx 2.2 \;\; \mu F \;\; (\text{use } 2.2 \text{ µF standard value})}$$

**Step 5 — Verify 2Δf_C < 2Δf_L:**
2Δf_C = 800 Hz, 2Δf_L = 8000 Hz ✓

**Final Design Summary:**

| Parameter | Designed Value |
|---|---|
| V_cc | +10 V |
| f_0 | 5 kHz |
| R₁ | 70 kΩ |
| C₁ | 0.001 µF |
| C₂ | 2.2 µF |
| R₂ | 3.6 kΩ (internal) |
| Δf_L achieved | ±4 kHz |
| Δf_C achieved | ±400 Hz |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat internal block diagram, describe the architecture of IC 565. Explain how the VCO free-running frequency is set by the external components R₁ and C₁. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an IC 565 PLL with R₁ = 10 kΩ, C₁ = 0.01 µF, and V_cc = +10 V, calculate (i) the free-running frequency, (ii) the lock range, and (iii) the value of C₂ required to obtain a capture range of 500 Hz. State the relationship between the capture range and the LPF time constant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**IC 565 Internal Architecture:**
The IC 565 consists of the following sub-blocks:
1. **Phase Detector:** A double-balanced multiplier-type PD with two differential inputs at Pins 11 and 12. It produces an output centred at V_cc/2 with a swing of ±V_cc/2.
2. **VCO:** A Schmitt-trigger-based astable multivibrator whose threshold voltages are V_cc/4 and 3V_cc/4, and whose timing is set by R₁ (Pin 8) and C₁ (Pin 9).
3. **Internal 3.6 kΩ biasing resistor** at Pin 7 for the LPF amplifier.
4. **LPF amplifier:** A high-gain inverting op-amp whose input is Pin 6 and output Pin 7. The external C₂ completes the LPF by connecting from Pin 7 to ground.

**VCO frequency setting:**
The VCO is a relaxation oscillator. C₁ charges through R₁ from V_cc/4 to 3V_cc/4, then discharges back to V_cc/4. The charge time is $t_c = R_1 C_1 \ln 3$. By symmetry, the discharge time is the same. Hence the period is $T_0 = 2 R_1 C_1 \ln 3$, giving:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2 R_1 C_1 \ln 3} = \frac{1.4}{4 R_1 C_1}$$

Thus R₁ and C₁ together set the free-running frequency. Doubling C₁ halves f_0; doubling R₁ also halves f_0.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Given:** R₁ = 10 kΩ, C₁ = 0.01 µF = 10⁻⁸ F, V_cc = 10 V.

**(i) Free-running frequency:**
$$f_0 = \frac{1.4}{4 R_1 C_1} = \frac{1.4}{4 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1.4}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 3500 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = 3.5 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(ii) Lock range:**
$$\Delta f_L = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{cc}} = \frac{8 \times 3500}{10} = 2800 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f_L = 2.8 \text{ kHz (one-sided)} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 2\Delta f_L = 5.6 \text{ kHz (total)}}$$

**(iii) Capture range C₂:**
Required Δf_C = 500 Hz, R₂ = 3.6 kΩ (internal).

$$500 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi \times 2800}{3600 \times C_2}}$$

$$(1000\pi)^2 = \frac{2\pi \times 2800}{3600 \, C_2}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{2\pi \times 2800}{3600 \times (1000\pi)^2} = \frac{2800}{3600 \times 1000^2 \times \pi} = \frac{2800}{1.131 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$C_2 \approx 2.48 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F} = 0.248 \;\; \mu F$$

Choose standard value:
$$\boxed{C_2 = 0.22 \;\; \mu F \;\; \text{(or } 0.33 \text{ µF for safer capture)}}$$

**Relationship between Capture Range and LPF Time Constant:**
The capture range is *inversely proportional* to the square root of the LPF time constant $\tau = R_2 C_2$:

$$\Delta f_C \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_2 C_2}}$$

A *narrower* LPF (larger τ) makes Δf_C *smaller* — the loop is sluggish to acquire but more immune to noise. A *wider* LPF (smaller τ) makes Δf_C *larger* but allows more noise to reach the VCO.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (where students commonly lose marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant 1.4 in the f_0 formula** — using $1/(4R_1C_1)$ instead of $1.4/(4R_1C_1)$ gives a 40 % error. The 1.4 comes from the factor 2 ln 3 inside the Schmitt astable.
> 2. **Mixing up total vs. one-sided ranges** — questions always ask for the *total* lock range, so multiply by 2. State explicitly "one-sided" or "total" to earn the method mark.
> 3. **Using the wrong constant in the capture formula** — some textbooks use $\sqrt{\Delta f_L / (2\pi R_2 C_2)}$; others use $\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{2\pi \Delta f_L / (R_2 C_2)}$. Both are correct; pick one and be consistent.
> 4. **Ignoring the 3.6 kΩ internal resistor** — for IC 565 specifically, R_2 in the capture formula is the *internal* 3.6 kΩ, not an external resistor the student dreams up.
> 5. **Confusing capture with lock during the experiment** — if the VCO output *stops following* the input, that is the lock limit; if the VCO output *suddenly starts following* the input as you sweep from far away, that is the capture limit. Note: the experiment has two separate sweeps.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- A **PLL** is a *negative feedback system in phase*; once locked, the VCO output frequency equals the input frequency and a small static phase error produces the control voltage.
- IC 565 contains **PD + VCO + LPF amplifier** in a 14-pin DIP. External components: only **R₁, C₁, R₂ (sometimes), C₂**.
- The three design equations to commit to memory:
  - $f_0 = 1.4 / (4 R_1 C_1)$
  - $\Delta f_L = 8 f_0 / V_{cc}$
  - $\Delta f_C = (1/2\pi) \sqrt{2\pi \Delta f_L / (R_2 C_2)}$ with $R_2 = 3.6 \text{ k}\Omega$
- The order is **always**: $\Delta f_C < \Delta f_L$ (capture is a subset of the lock band).
- Practical design limits: **R₁** between 2 kΩ and 100 kΩ; **C₁** between 0.001 µF and 10 µF; **V_cc** between ±6 V and ±12 V.
- The **PD output** is centred at V_cc/2; the **VCO output** is a square wave at f_0 ± K_0 V_c; the **LPF output** (Pin 7) is the control voltage.
- Experimentally, distinguish capture from lock: capture = "first lock from unlocked"; lock = "still in lock after wandering".
- Pin 11 = input, Pin 12 = VCO feedback, Pin 4 = VCO output, Pin 7 = LPF output (= VCO control voltage).
- Don't forget to **decouple** V_cc (Pin 1) to ground with a 0.1 µF ceramic capacitor placed close to the IC — this is a frequent omission in student lab kits and causes the PLL to misbehave at higher frequencies.
- The **VCO sensitivity** K_0 numerically equals Δf_L (in Hz/V).
- The **PD sensitivity** of IC 565 is approximately $K_d \approx 1.4/\pi$ V/rad — built-in and unchangeable.
- For laboratory verification, plot **f_out vs f_in** (s-curve) and **V_PD vs f_in** (triangular) — both demonstrate the lock and capture limits graphically.
